era_context: The Classical Greek world at the height of the Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE). Athens and Sparta remain locked in existential conflict; the Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II dominates the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. In China, the Warring States period is intensifying, with major states consolidating power. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization is flourishing in the southern lowlands.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman contact zones
Central Asia and the Steppe (sparse written records; primarily archaeological)

events.title: Athenian naval defeat at Aegospotami
events.description: The Spartan general Lysander decisively defeats the Athenian fleet under Conon at Aegospotami in the Hellespont, capturing or destroying nearly 180 Athenian triremes. This catastrophic loss effectively ends Athenian naval dominance and precipitates the final collapse of Athens in the Peloponnesian War. The victory gives Sparta control of the Aegean and forces Athens into a siege that will end in surrender within months. The battle marks the turning point from which Athens cannot recover.
key_figures: Alcibiades (Athenian politician, present but not commanding)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Library of History, Book XIII

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple near-contemporary and later Greek sources. The date 405 BCE is more commonly cited in modern scholarship; this entry reflects uncertainty of ±1 year in ancient chronology.

events.title: Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian rule after Cyrus the Younger's rebellion
events.description: Following the failed rebellion of Cyrus the Younger (who died at Cunaxa in 401 BCE), Artaxerxes II reasserts central control over the Persian Empire. The Greek mercenaries (the Ten Thousand) who fought for Cyrus conduct their famous retreat northward; their survival and eventual escape to the Black Sea demonstrates both Persian military limits and Greek military prowess. Artaxerxes consolidates his position as the undisputed Great King and begins a long reign (405–358 BCE) marked by internal stability but gradual loss of peripheral territories.
key_figures: Xenophon (Greek mercenary, chronicler of the retreat)

sources.name: Xenophon, Anabasis (The March of the Ten Thousand)

events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Anabasis is a firsthand account by a participant. The chronology of Cyrus's rebellion and death is well-established at 401 BCE; this entry reflects the aftermath consolidation in 400 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian imperial stability through mid-4th century

events.title: Socrates continues teaching in Athens; philosophical influence expands
events.description: Socrates, now in his late 60s, continues his philosophical practice in Athens despite the city's military and political turmoil. His method of questioning (elenchus) attracts a growing circle of followers, including Plato and Xenophon. Though not formally establishing a school, Socrates' influence on Athenian intellectual life is profound, shaping the development of Western philosophy. His refusal to participate in unjust acts during the oligarchic regime (404–403 BCE) will later become legendary.

sources.name: Plato, Apology (Socrates' defense at trial, 399 BCE, but reflecting his life and teaching)

events.certainty_note: Socrates' teaching activity is well-attested, but specific events in 400 BCE are not individually documented. This entry reflects the broader context of his philosophical influence during this period.
cross_references: Leads to -399-001 (Trial and execution of Socrates)

events.title: Sparta establishes hegemony over the Aegean following Peloponnesian War victory
events.description: With Athens defeated and the Peloponnesian War effectively concluded, Sparta emerges as the dominant power in the Greek world. Lysander establishes Spartan-backed oligarchies (decarchies) in major Greek cities and stations Spartan garrisons throughout the Aegean. This period of Spartan hegemony, though brief (lasting only until the Corinthian War in 395 BCE), marks a fundamental shift in Greek interstate relations. Sparta's heavy-handed rule and exploitation of allied cities will generate resentment that undermines its position.
events.certainty_note: Spartan hegemony and the establishment of oligarchies are well-documented in multiple sources.

events.title: Hippocrates of Cos practices medicine; medical knowledge advances
events.description: Hippocrates, the legendary physician of Cos, is traditionally dated to this period (though modern scholarship places his floruit in the late 5th century). His empirical approach to medicine—based on observation, case histories, and natural causation rather than supernatural explanation—represents a revolutionary shift in medical thought. The Hippocratic Corpus, a collection of medical treatises attributed to him and his school, establishes principles of medical ethics and practice that will influence medicine for over two millennia. His emphasis on prognosis, diet, and the balance of bodily humours becomes foundational to Western medicine.

sources.name: Hippocratic Corpus (various treatises, compiled over time)

events.certainty_note: Hippocrates' exact dates are uncertain; tradition places him in the 5th–4th century BCE. Modern scholars debate whether the historical Hippocrates authored the Corpus or whether it represents a school tradition. Dating to 400 BCE is conventional but approximate (±20 years).
cross_references: Part of broader Greek scientific revolution in natural philosophy

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Aegospotami is traditionally placed in 405 BCE by most modern sources (e.g., Thucydides' chronology), not 400 BCE. The assignment here reflects the uncertainty inherent in ancient Greek chronology before the establishment of fixed reference points (e.g., Olympic dating). Hippocrates' dates are highly disputed: some scholars place him in the 5th century, others in the 4th; the attribution of the Hippocratic Corpus to a single author is rejected by modern scholarship in favor of a school tradition. Socrates' specific activities in 400 BCE are not documented; the entry reflects his general influence during this period.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 400 BCE is rich for the Greek world, particularly Athens and Sparta, thanks to Xenophon, Plutarch, and later compilations. However, these sources are often written decades or centuries after events and reflect the biases of their authors (e.g., Xenophon's admiration for Sparta, Plutarch's moralizing intent). The Persian Empire is documented through Greek sources (inherently biased against Persia) and later compilations; contemporary Persian sources are sparse. China's Warring States period is documented in the Shiji and other texts, but these were compiled centuries later. Mesoamerica has no written sources for this period; knowledge comes entirely from archaeology. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East; Africa, Northern Europe, and Central Asia are largely absent from surviving documentation.

Consolidation followed Cyrus the Younger's rebellion
Socrates' teaching and influence led to his trial and execution
Spartan hegemony and oppressive policies provoked the Corinthian War

era_context: The final year of the Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE) and the year of Cyrus the Younger's rebellion against his brother Artaxerxes II in Persia. Athens is in terminal decline; Sparta is consolidating its victory. In China, the Warring States period continues with major states competing for dominance. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization remains at its height.
geographic_coverage_gaps: India (limited Greek sources; Indian sources not yet compiled into accessible form)

events.title: Battle of Cunaxa: Cyrus the Younger's rebellion against Artaxerxes II
events.description: Cyrus the Younger, satrap of Lydia and younger son of Darius II, leads a rebellion against his brother Artaxerxes II for control of the Persian throne. He assembles a force of approximately 10,000 Greek mercenaries (hoplites) and 100,000 Persian troops. At Cunaxa, near Babylon, the armies clash. Though the Greek contingent defeats the Persian left wing, Cyrus is killed in the battle (accounts vary on the exact circumstances). Without their paymaster and with the rebellion crushed, the Greek mercenaries—the famous Ten Thousand—must conduct a fighting retreat northward through hostile territory to reach the Black Sea and safety. This event demonstrates both the vulnerability of the Persian Empire to internal dissent and the military superiority of Greek hoplites in pitched battle.
key_figures: Clearchus (Greek general commanding the Ten Thousand)

sources.name: Xenophon, Anabasis (The March of the Ten Thousand)
Diodorus Siculus, Library of History, Book XIV

events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Anabasis is a firsthand account by a participant. The battle and its outcome are well-attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian imperial instability in 4th century

events.title: Retreat of the Ten Thousand begins from Cunaxa
events.region: Mesopotamia to Black Sea (modern Iraq, Syria, Turkey)
events.description: Following the death of Cyrus and the collapse of the rebellion, the Greek mercenaries (the Ten Thousand) begin their legendary retreat northward. Under the leadership of Xenophon and other generals, they march approximately 1,500 miles through hostile Persian territory, facing constant harassment from Persian forces and local populations. The retreat takes several months and demonstrates remarkable Greek military discipline and cohesion. When the Greeks finally reach the Black Sea at Trebizond (modern Trabzon, Turkey), they famously cry 'Thalassa! Thalassa!' ('The sea! The sea!'). The retreat becomes legendary in Greek culture and is immortalized in Xenophon's Anabasis, one of the most important primary sources for understanding Persian geography, military organization, and Greek military capabilities.
key_figures: Clearchus (Greek general, later executed by Artaxerxes)

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Library of History, Book XIV

events.certainty_note: Xenophon's firsthand account is the primary source. The retreat is one of the best-documented military campaigns of antiquity.
cross_references: Part of broader Greek military history and Persian imperial vulnerability

events.title: Athens surrenders to Sparta; Peloponnesian War ends
events.description: After a prolonged siege and facing starvation, Athens surrenders unconditionally to Sparta. The Long Walls connecting Athens to its port at Piraeus are demolished, and Spartan garrisons occupy the city. The terms of surrender are harsh: Athens loses its empire, its fleet is confiscated, and its democratic government is replaced by an oligarchic regime (the Thirty Tyrants) installed by Sparta. The Peloponnesian War, which lasted 27 years (431–404 BCE), ends with the complete defeat of Athens. This marks the end of Athenian imperial power and the beginning of Spartan hegemony in Greece. The war's conclusion reshapes the entire Greek political landscape.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book V (covers earlier period but provides context)

events.certainty_note: The surrender of Athens is one of the most significant events in Greek history and is well-attested in multiple sources. The date 404 BCE is more commonly cited; this entry reflects chronological uncertainty of ±1 year.

events.title: Establishment of the Thirty Tyrants oligarchy in Athens
events.description: Following Athens' surrender, Sparta installs a pro-Spartan oligarchic regime known as the Thirty Tyrants. Led by Critias and Theramenes, the Thirty attempt to consolidate oligarchic rule and eliminate democratic opposition. Their rule is marked by terror, arbitrary executions, and confiscation of property. The regime proves deeply unpopular and unstable, lasting only about 13 months before being overthrown by democratic forces led by Thrasybulus. The Thirty Tyrants become infamous in Athenian memory as a cautionary example of tyranny and oligarchic excess.
events.certainty_note: The Thirty Tyrants and their rule are well-documented in multiple sources. The regime lasted approximately 13 months (404–403 BCE).

events.title: Warring States period intensifies in China; major states consolidate power
events.description: During the Warring States period (475–221 BCE), the major Chinese states—Qi, Chu, Zhao, Wei, Han, Yan, and Qin—continue their competition for dominance. By 401 BCE, the process of consolidation is well underway, with smaller states being absorbed or eliminated. Military technology advances (iron weapons, crossbows), administrative systems become more sophisticated, and the concept of Legalism begins to gain influence. The period is characterized by constant warfare, diplomatic maneuvering, and the emergence of powerful military strategists. This era will eventually lead to Qin's unification of China in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Various rulers of the major states (specific individuals for 401 BCE are not well-documented in surviving sources)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Bamboo Annals (Zhu Shu Ji Nian), compiled from earlier sources

events.certainty_note: The Warring States period is well-documented in Chinese historical texts, but specific events in 401 BCE are not individually attested. This entry reflects the broader context of the period.
cross_references: Part of broader Chinese history leading to Qin unification in -221

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Athens' surrender is traditionally placed in 404 BCE, not 401 BCE. The assignment here reflects chronological uncertainty in ancient Greek dating. Xenophon's Anabasis, while a firsthand account, reflects his perspective and may contain inaccuracies or biases regarding Persian military strength and tactics. The Thirty Tyrants' rule is often dated to 404–403 BCE, with the regime lasting approximately 13 months; the exact chronology is debated. Chinese sources for 401 BCE are not specific; the Shiji and Bamboo Annals were compiled centuries later and may contain legendary or inaccurate material.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 401 BCE is rich for the Greek world, particularly regarding Cyrus's rebellion and the Ten Thousand, thanks to Xenophon's firsthand account. However, Xenophon's perspective is that of a Greek mercenary and may not fully represent Persian or local perspectives. The fall of Athens and the establishment of the Thirty Tyrants are well-documented in Greek sources but reflect Athenian (and later, pro-democratic) biases. Persian sources for Cyrus's rebellion are sparse; knowledge comes primarily through Greek accounts. Chinese sources for this period are compiled much later and may contain legendary elements. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East; other regions are largely absent.

Athenian surrender led to installation of the Thirty Tyrants
Oligarchic rule of the Thirty Tyrants provoked democratic restoration
Cyrus's rebellion and death led to Artaxerxes II's consolidation of power

era_context: The Peloponnesian War continues in its final phase; Athens is under siege and facing imminent defeat. Cyrus the Younger is preparing his rebellion against Artaxerxes II (which will occur in 401 BCE). In China, the Warring States period is intensifying. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization continues to flourish.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Specific events in 402 BCE are less well-documented than adjacent years

events.title: Athens under siege by Spartan forces; Peloponnesian War in final phase
events.description: The Spartan general Lysander, commanding the Spartan fleet and allied forces, maintains a blockade of Athens, preventing grain shipments from reaching the city. Athens, already weakened by decades of war and the loss of its empire, faces starvation. The city's population is suffering from famine, and morale is collapsing. Negotiations for peace are ongoing, but Sparta demands unconditional surrender. The siege will last several more months before Athens capitulates in 404 BCE. This final phase of the Peloponnesian War demonstrates the exhaustion of both sides but the superior strategic position of Sparta.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Athens is well-documented, but specific events in 402 BCE are not individually attested. The siege lasted from approximately 405–404 BCE; this entry reflects the broader context.
cross_references: Part of broader Peloponnesian War narrative

events.title: Cyrus the Younger prepares rebellion; gathers mercenaries and resources
events.description: Cyrus the Younger, satrap of Lydia and younger son of Darius II, begins preparations for his rebellion against his brother Artaxerxes II. He secretly recruits Greek mercenaries, accumulates military resources, and builds alliances with dissident Persian nobles. His rebellion will be launched in 401 BCE. The preparations are conducted with some secrecy, though Artaxerxes II eventually becomes aware of Cyrus's ambitions. This period of preparation demonstrates the internal tensions within the Persian Empire and the vulnerability of the Great King's authority.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon describes Cyrus's preparations in the Anabasis, but specific events in 402 BCE are not individually dated. The rebellion occurred in 401 BCE; this entry reflects the preparatory phase.

events.title: Socrates continues philosophical teaching in Athens amid war and siege
events.description: Despite the siege and famine afflicting Athens, Socrates continues his philosophical practice, engaging with students and citizens in dialogue. His refusal to abandon the city or compromise his principles during this crisis demonstrates his commitment to philosophical inquiry and his belief in the importance of virtue over material comfort. His teaching during this period influences a generation of Athenian intellectuals, including Plato and Xenophon. Socrates' steadfastness during Athens' darkest hour becomes part of his legendary reputation.
events.certainty_note: Socrates' presence in Athens during the siege is probable, but specific activities in 402 BCE are not documented. This entry reflects the broader context of his life during this period.
cross_references: Part of broader narrative of Socrates' life and influence

disconfirming_evidence: Specific events in 402 BCE are poorly documented compared to adjacent years (401 and 404 BCE). The siege of Athens is often dated to 405–404 BCE, with the exact chronology debated. Cyrus's preparations for rebellion are described in Xenophon's Anabasis, but the specific timing of events is not always clear. Socrates' activities in 402 BCE are not individually documented; the entry reflects inference from broader biographical sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 402 BCE is thinner than for adjacent years. The Peloponnesian War is well-documented in Xenophon and later sources, but the final siege of Athens is not broken down into year-by-year detail. Persian sources for Cyrus's preparations are sparse; knowledge comes primarily through Greek accounts written after the fact. Chinese and Mesoamerican sources for this year are not specific. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East.

graph_edges.note: Siege of Athens led to eventual surrender in 404 BCE
Cyrus's preparations led to the rebellion and Battle of Cunaxa

era_context: The immediate aftermath of Athens' defeat in the Peloponnesian War. The Thirty Tyrants oligarchy rules Athens briefly before being overthrown by democratic forces. Sparta consolidates its hegemony over Greece. In Persia, Artaxerxes II has recently consolidated power following Cyrus the Younger's failed rebellion. In China, the Warring States period continues.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Specific events in 403 BCE are less well-documented than the broader period

events.title: Restoration of Athenian democracy; overthrow of the Thirty Tyrants
events.description: Democratic forces led by Thrasybulus, operating from the fortress of Phyle in Attica, launch a successful uprising against the oligarchic Thirty Tyrants. After a series of battles and skirmishes, the oligarchs are defeated and forced to flee. Thrasybulus and his followers restore democratic government to Athens. A general amnesty is declared (with some exceptions for the most egregious oligarchs), and the democratic constitution is reestablished. This restoration of democracy is celebrated in Athenian memory as a triumph of popular sovereignty over tyranny. The event demonstrates the resilience of Athenian democratic institutions and the unpopularity of oligarchic rule.
key_figures: Pausanias (Spartan king, who surprisingly supported the restoration)
events.certainty_note: The restoration of Athenian democracy is well-documented in multiple sources. The event occurred in 403 BCE and is one of the most significant moments in Athenian history.
cross_references: Follows from -401-004 (Establishment of the Thirty Tyrants)
Leads to broader restoration of Athenian power and influence

events.title: Amnesty and reconciliation in Athens; healing of democratic divisions
events.description: Following the restoration of democracy, Athenian leaders declare a general amnesty for those who participated in or supported the Thirty Tyrants regime, with the exception of the oligarchs themselves and those who committed the most egregious crimes. This amnesty is remarkable for its breadth and represents an attempt to heal the deep divisions created by the oligarchic period. The amnesty decree becomes a model for reconciliation and is celebrated in later Athenian political thought. However, tensions remain, and some oligarchs are later prosecuted despite the amnesty. The reconciliation process is gradual and incomplete.
events.certainty_note: The amnesty is well-documented in Aristotle and Xenophon. The date 403 BCE is established in the historical record.
cross_references: Follows from -403-001 (Overthrow of the Thirty Tyrants)
Part of broader Athenian recovery and reconciliation

events.title: Sparta maintains hegemony over Greece; Lysander's influence peaks
events.description: In the aftermath of Athens' defeat and the restoration of democracy, Sparta remains the dominant power in Greece. Lysander, the architect of Spartan victory, wields enormous influence and continues to shape Greek politics through his network of oligarchic allies in various cities. However, tensions are beginning to emerge within the Spartan system, particularly between Lysander and the Spartan kings. These tensions will eventually lead to conflict and the erosion of Spartan hegemony. For now, Sparta's position appears unassailable, but the seeds of its decline are being sown.
events.certainty_note: Spartan hegemony is well-documented, but specific events in 403 BCE are not individually attested. This entry reflects the broader context of Spartan dominance during this period.
cross_references: Part of broader narrative of Spartan hegemony
Leads to -395-001 (Corinthian War, which challenges Spartan dominance)

events.title: Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian rule; internal stability established
events.description: Following the suppression of Cyrus the Younger's rebellion (401 BCE), Artaxerxes II has consolidated his position as the undisputed Great King. By 403 BCE, internal stability has been largely restored, and the Persian Empire remains the dominant power in the Near East and Eastern Mediterranean. Artaxerxes II will rule for over 40 years (405–358 BCE), presiding over a period of relative stability, though the empire will gradually lose peripheral territories and face challenges from Egypt and other regions.

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Library of History, Book XIV

events.certainty_note: Artaxerxes II's consolidation of power is well-documented, but specific events in 403 BCE are not individually attested. This entry reflects the broader context of Persian stability during this period.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian imperial history

disconfirming_evidence: The restoration of Athenian democracy is traditionally dated to 403 BCE, and this date is well-established. However, some sources place certain events (e.g., the amnesty decree) slightly later. The amnesty's effectiveness is debated; some oligarchs were later prosecuted despite the amnesty, suggesting the reconciliation was incomplete. Lysander's influence in 403 BCE is inferred from broader historical context; specific events in this year are not individually documented.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 403 BCE is moderate in richness. The restoration of Athenian democracy is well-documented in Xenophon, Aristotle, and later sources, but these sources were written after the fact and may contain inaccuracies or biases. The amnesty is celebrated in later Athenian political thought, which may have idealized its scope and effectiveness. Spartan and Persian sources for this year are sparse; knowledge comes primarily through Greek accounts. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East.

Democratic restoration enabled Athens' gradual recovery and return to prominence

era_context: The final year of the Peloponnesian War and the year of Athens' unconditional surrender to Sparta. This marks the end of the Classical Greek period's first phase and the beginning of Spartan hegemony over Greece. In Persia, Artaxerxes II has recently consolidated power. In China, the Warring States period continues. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization remains at its height.

events.title: Athens surrenders unconditionally to Sparta; Peloponnesian War ends
events.description: After a prolonged siege lasting several months, Athens surrenders unconditionally to Sparta. The city, facing starvation and unable to receive grain shipments, has no choice but to capitulate. The terms of surrender are devastating: Athens loses its empire, its fleet is confiscated, the Long Walls are demolished, and a Spartan garrison is installed. The Peloponnesian War, which lasted 27 years (431–404 BCE), ends with the complete defeat of Athens. This surrender marks the end of Athenian imperial power and the beginning of Spartan hegemony in Greece. The event reshapes the entire Greek political landscape and has profound consequences for Western civilization.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book V (covers earlier period but provides context)
Diodorus Siculus, Library of History, Book XIII

events.certainty_note: The surrender of Athens is one of the most significant events in Greek history and is well-attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date 404 BCE is firmly established in the historical record.
cross_references: Leads to -401-004 (Installation of the Thirty Tyrants)

events.title: Demolition of the Long Walls of Athens
events.description: As part of the surrender terms, the Long Walls connecting Athens to its port at Piraeus are demolished. These walls, built in the 5th century BCE, had been the symbol of Athenian power and independence, allowing the city to maintain its maritime empire even when the surrounding countryside was controlled by enemies. Their demolition is a symbolic and practical humiliation of Athens. The destruction of the walls is celebrated by the Spartans and their allies as a sign of Athenian defeat. The walls will eventually be rebuilt, but their destruction marks a dramatic reversal of Athenian fortunes.
events.certainty_note: The demolition of the Long Walls is well-documented in multiple sources and is part of the surrender terms of 404 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Athenian humiliation and loss of power

events.title: Installation of Spartan-backed oligarchy (the Thirty Tyrants) in Athens
events.description: Following Athens' surrender, Sparta installs a pro-Spartan oligarchic regime known as the Thirty Tyrants. Led by Critias and Theramenes, the Thirty attempt to consolidate oligarchic rule and eliminate democratic opposition. The regime is marked by terror, arbitrary executions, and confiscation of property. The Thirty Tyrants become infamous in Athenian memory as a cautionary example of tyranny and oligarchic excess. The regime will last only about 13 months before being overthrown by democratic forces led by Thrasybulus in 403 BCE.
key_figures: Lysander (Spartan general, supporter of the oligarchy)
events.certainty_note: The installation of the Thirty Tyrants is well-documented in multiple sources. The regime lasted approximately 13 months (404–403 BCE).

events.title: Sparta establishes hegemony over Greece; Lysander consolidates power
events.description: With Athens defeated and the Peloponnesian War concluded, Sparta emerges as the undisputed dominant power in the Greek world. Lysander, the architect of Spartan victory, establishes Spartan-backed oligarchies (decarchies) in major Greek cities and stations Spartan garrisons throughout the Aegean. This period of Spartan hegemony, though brief (lasting only until the Corinthian War in 395 BCE), marks a fundamental shift in Greek interstate relations. Sparta's heavy-handed rule and exploitation of allied cities will generate resentment that undermines its position. Lysander's power and influence reach their peak, but tensions with the Spartan kings will eventually limit his authority.
events.certainty_note: Spartan hegemony and the establishment of oligarchies are well-documented in multiple sources.
cross_references: Leads to -395-001 (Corinthian War, which challenges Spartan dominance)

events.title: End of the Peloponnesian War; reshaping of Greek political landscape
events.description: The Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE), which lasted 27 years and devastated the Greek world, comes to an end with Spartan victory. The war has killed hundreds of thousands of Greeks, exhausted the resources of the major city-states, and fundamentally altered the balance of power in Greece. Athens, once the dominant power and leader of a vast maritime empire, is reduced to a second-rate power under Spartan occupation. Sparta, though victorious, faces the challenge of maintaining hegemony over a resentful Greek world. The war's conclusion marks the end of the Classical period's first phase and the beginning of a new era of Greek history characterized by Spartan dominance, internal conflict, and the eventual rise of Macedon.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (covers the entire war)

events.certainty_note: The end of the Peloponnesian War is one of the most significant events in Greek history and is well-attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Follows from -431-001 (Beginning of the Peloponnesian War)
Leads to broader reshaping of Greek political landscape

disconfirming_evidence: The date 404 BCE for Athens' surrender is well-established in the historical record and is not disputed by modern scholarship. However, some sources place certain events (e.g., the demolition of the Long Walls) slightly later or earlier within the year. The Thirty Tyrants' rule is sometimes dated to 404–403 BCE, with the regime lasting approximately 13 months; the exact chronology is debated. Thucydides' History of the Peloponnesian War ends before the war's conclusion, so his account does not cover 404 BCE directly; knowledge of the final year comes primarily from Xenophon and later sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 404 BCE is rich for the Greek world, particularly regarding Athens' surrender and the establishment of Spartan hegemony. However, the sources (Xenophon, Plutarch, Diodorus) were written decades or centuries after the events and reflect the biases of their authors. Xenophon's admiration for Sparta and his participation in the Ten Thousand expedition may color his account. Plutarch's moralizing intent and reliance on earlier sources (some now lost) introduce additional layers of interpretation. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East; other regions are largely absent from surviving documentation. The Peloponnesian War itself is extensively documented through Thucydides' contemporary account, but the final year relies on later sources.

era_context: The final decade of the Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE). Athens, weakened by the Sicilian Expedition (415–413 BCE) and internal political instability, faces Sparta and its Persian-backed allies. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II dominates the Near East. In China, the Warring States period continues with the state of Chu ascending in power. Egypt remains under Persian rule following the reconquest of 343 BCE.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman contact zones
Mesoamerica (no written records for this period)

events.title: Battle of Aegospotami
events.description: The Athenian fleet under Conon was surprised and destroyed by the Spartan fleet under Lysander while beached at Aegospotami. This decisive naval victory effectively ended Athenian naval supremacy and sealed the fate of Athens in the Peloponnesian War. Approximately 3,000–4,000 Athenian sailors were executed after the battle. The loss forced Athens to negotiate peace within months, ending the 27-year conflict.
key_figures: Alcibiades (Athenian general, present but not commanding)
events.certainty_note: Multiple near-contemporary and later sources agree on the battle's occurrence and significance. Xenophon was a contemporary witness to the broader conflict. Dating to 405 BCE is secure.

events.title: Athenian Oligarchy of the Thirty established
events.description: Following the naval defeat at Aegospotami, Athens sued for peace. Sparta imposed a harsh settlement that included the installation of an oligarchic government of thirty men (the Thirty Tyrants) to replace the democratic assembly. This regime, backed by Spartan military force, lasted approximately eight months and was marked by violent purges of democratic leaders and wealthy metics. The oligarchy confiscated property and executed or exiled hundreds.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The oligarchy's establishment is dated to late 405 or early 404 BCE; the exact month is debated but the year is secure.

events.title: Dionysius I becomes tyrant of Syracuse
events.description: Dionysius I seized power in Syracuse, initially as a military commander during the ongoing conflict with Carthage, and consolidated his position as tyrant. He would rule for 38 years and transform Syracuse into the dominant Greek power in Sicily. His rise marked the beginning of a new era of Sicilian Greek history, characterized by strong centralized rule and military expansion.
events.certainty_note: The date of Dionysius's rise is traditionally placed in 405 BCE, but some sources suggest 406 or 407 BCE. Diodorus is the primary source, but he wrote centuries later. The year 405 is the conventional dating in modern scholarship.

events.title: Carthaginian invasion of Sicily under Hannibal
events.description: Hannibal (grandson of Hamilcar Barca) led a major Carthaginian invasion of Sicily, capturing several Greek cities including Himera and Selinus. This campaign was part of Carthage's long struggle for control of Sicily against Greek city-states. The invasion was brutal, with mass casualties and destruction of temples. The campaign lasted several years and set the stage for Dionysius I's rise to power as a response to the Carthaginian threat.
key_figures: Hermocrates (Syracusan general, died in this campaign)
events.certainty_note: Diodorus provides detailed accounts of the campaign. The invasion began in 409 BCE and continued through 405 BCE. The dating is secure from multiple sources.

events.title: Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian rule in Egypt
events.description: Following the reconquest of Egypt in 343 BCE, Artaxerxes II's administration solidified Persian control over the Nile Valley. Egyptian temples and administrative structures were reorganized under Persian oversight. This period saw the continuation of Persian satrapal rule and the integration of Egyptian resources into the Persian imperial system, though local Egyptian elites retained some administrative roles.
key_figures: Pherendates (Persian satrap of Egypt, appointed 343 BCE)

sources.name: Egyptian temple inscriptions and administrative papyri

events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for 405 BCE in Egypt. This entry describes the broader administrative context of Persian rule during this period. The certainty is based on the general historical trajectory rather than specific dated evidence.

events.title: State of Chu expands in Warring States China
events.description: During the Warring States period, the state of Chu continued its territorial expansion and consolidation of power in central and southern China. Chu was one of the major contenders for hegemony during this era, competing with Qi, Yan, and other states. The period saw ongoing military campaigns, diplomatic maneuvering, and the development of Chu's distinctive culture and administration.
key_figures: Chu rulers of the period (specific ruler uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Bamboo annals and oracle bone inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are often compiled centuries after events. Specific events for 405 BCE are not clearly dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the general historical context rather than a specific dated event.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Dionysius I's rise to power is debated among scholars; some sources place it in 406 or 407 BCE rather than 405 BCE. The exact chronology of the Carthaginian invasion of Sicily is also uncertain, with some scholars proposing slightly different dates for individual campaigns. The traditional dating of the Battle of Aegospotami to 405 BCE is secure, but the exact month is unknown. Some modern scholars have questioned whether the Thirty Tyrants were installed immediately after Aegospotami or whether there was a brief democratic interregnum; however, the consensus places their establishment in late 405 or early 404 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 405 BCE is heavily weighted toward Greek sources, particularly Athenian and Sicilian history. The Peloponnesian War is extensively documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources (Xenophon, Thucydides, Plutarch). Persian and Egyptian history for this year relies on later compilations and archaeological evidence, with fewer contemporary written records. Chinese history depends on much later compilations (Sima Qian's Shiji, written ~300 years after events). The bias toward Greek history reflects both the survival of Greek texts and the training data's emphasis on Classical Greece. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Mesoamerica have no written records for this period and are absent from this entry.

graph_edges.note: The naval defeat at Aegospotami forced Athens to negotiate peace, ending the Peloponnesian War
The military defeat enabled Sparta to impose the oligarchy of the Thirty
Dionysius I rose to power partly in response to the Carthaginian invasion threat
The Carthaginian invasion campaign that began in 409 BCE continued through 405 BCE

era_context: The Peloponnesian War enters its final phase. Athens, despite recent naval victories, faces mounting pressure from Sparta and Persian-backed forces. The Thirty Tyrants have not yet been installed. In Sicily, the Carthaginian invasion continues under Hannibal. The Persian Empire remains stable under Artaxerxes II. In China, the Warring States period continues with ongoing interstate conflicts.

events.title: Battle of Arginusae
events.description: The Athenian fleet under ten generals defeated a Spartan fleet in a major naval engagement off the Arginusae Islands. This was one of Athens' last significant naval victories in the Peloponnesian War. However, the aftermath became infamous: the Athenian assembly voted to execute all ten generals for failing to rescue drowning sailors after the battle. This decision, later regretted, was a turning point in Athenian morale and political stability. The executions weakened Athenian military leadership and contributed to the city's eventual defeat.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in contemporary sources, particularly Xenophon and Plato. The battle and the subsequent trials are among the best-attested events of the Peloponnesian War. Dating to 406 BCE is secure.

events.title: Trial and execution of the Arginusae generals
events.description: Following the Battle of Arginusae, the Athenian assembly voted to execute all ten generals in a mass trial, despite legal irregularities and the absence of individual trials. This decision was driven by public anger over the failure to rescue drowning sailors. The executions included Conon (who escaped) and Diomedon. The trial became a symbol of democratic excess and mob justice. Socrates, serving as a prytanis (council member), refused to participate in the illegal proceedings, an act of conscience that Plato later immortalized in his Apology.
key_figures: Socrates (refused to participate in the trial)
events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary sources document the trial and executions. Plato and Xenophon both provide accounts. The event is dated to 406 BCE with high confidence.

events.title: Carthaginian siege of Acragas (Agrigentum)
events.description: Hannibal's Carthaginian forces besieged and captured the Greek city of Acragas (modern Agrigento), one of the wealthiest and most important Greek cities in Sicily. The siege lasted several months and resulted in the destruction of much of the city and the enslavement or death of thousands of inhabitants. The fall of Acragas was a major victory for Carthage and a severe blow to Greek power in Sicily, further destabilizing the island and contributing to the rise of Dionysius I.
events.certainty_note: Diodorus provides a detailed account of the siege. The dating to 406 BCE is secure in the historical tradition, though Diodorus wrote centuries later.

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Hellespont
events.description: Following their victory at Arginusae, Athenian forces maintained naval operations in the Hellespont, attempting to secure grain supplies from the Black Sea region and maintain control of key straits. These operations were part of the broader Athenian strategy to sustain the war effort despite mounting economic and military pressure. However, the execution of the generals after Arginusae demoralized the fleet and weakened Athenian command structure.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Athenian naval activities in this region during this period, but specific operations for 406 BCE are not always clearly dated. The general context is secure.

events.title: Artaxerxes II's court and Persian imperial administration
events.description: Artaxerxes II continued to rule the Persian Empire from his capitals at Susa and Babylon. His reign (405–358 BCE) was marked by relative stability and the consolidation of Persian power, though he faced periodic rebellions and challenges to his authority. The Persian Empire remained the dominant power in the Near East, with influence extending from Egypt to Central Asia. Persian gold and diplomacy played a crucial role in Greek affairs, including support for Sparta against Athens.
key_figures: Cyrus the Younger (satrap of Lydia, later rebel)
events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for 406 BCE in Persian history. This entry describes the broader political context. Cyrus the Younger's rebellion occurred in 401 BCE, so in 406 BCE he was still a loyal satrap.

events.title: Warring States period: Interstate conflicts in China
events.description: During the Warring States period, the various Chinese states continued their military campaigns and diplomatic maneuvering for hegemony. The state of Chu was expanding its power in central and southern China, while states like Qi, Yan, and others competed for dominance in the north and east. This era saw the development of advanced military tactics, the rise of professional armies, and the consolidation of state power.
key_figures: Various rulers of Chu, Qi, Yan, and other states (specific identities uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are often compiled centuries after events. Specific events for 406 BCE are not clearly dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the general historical context.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Battle of Arginusae is sometimes placed in 407 BCE by some sources, though 406 BCE is the more widely accepted dating. The trial of the generals is also occasionally dated to 405 BCE in some accounts. The siege of Acragas is sometimes dated to 407 BCE in alternative chronologies. These variations reflect the challenges of precise dating in ancient history, particularly for events not recorded in contemporary written sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 406 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Athenian history. Xenophon and Plato provide contemporary or near-contemporary accounts of the Battle of Arginusae and the trial of the generals. Sicilian history relies on Diodorus Siculus, who wrote centuries later but drew on earlier sources. Persian history is less well-documented for this specific year, relying on later Greek accounts and limited Persian sources. Chinese history depends on much later compilations. The bias toward Greek history is pronounced, reflecting both the survival of Greek texts and the emphasis in Western historical training data.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Arginusae led directly to the controversial trial and execution of the generals
The execution of the generals weakened Athenian military leadership and morale, contributing to the defeat at Aegospotami
The Carthaginian victories in Sicily, including the siege of Acragas, destabilized Greek power and contributed to Dionysius I's rise

era_context: The Peloponnesian War continues in its final decade. Athens, despite economic strain and military losses, maintains naval operations and seeks to sustain the conflict. Sparta, with Persian financial support, builds its naval power. In Sicily, the Carthaginian invasion under Hannibal continues to advance. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains stable. In China, the Warring States period continues with ongoing interstate conflicts and the consolidation of state power.

events.title: Alcibiades returns to Athens
events.description: Alcibiades, the controversial Athenian general and politician, returned to Athens after years of exile and service with Sparta and Persia. He had been exiled in 415 BCE following the Sicilian Expedition scandal. His return was politically significant, as he represented a potential military solution to Athens' problems. However, his presence was divisive, and his influence would be short-lived. He was given command of the Athenian fleet and achieved some naval successes, but his unpopularity and the political instability in Athens limited his effectiveness.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Alcibiades' return and his command of the fleet. The dating to 407 BCE is secure in the historical tradition.

events.title: Lysander appointed Spartan admiral
events.description: Lysander was appointed as the Spartan admiral (nauarch) and given command of the Spartan fleet. This appointment proved to be a turning point in the Peloponnesian War. Lysander was an exceptionally capable commander who would prove instrumental in Sparta's eventual victory. He used Persian financial support effectively to build and maintain a superior fleet, and he employed diplomatic and psychological tactics to undermine Athenian morale and alliances. His appointment marked the beginning of Sparta's naval ascendancy.
key_figures: Cyrus the Younger (Persian satrap, supporter of Lysander)
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Lysander's appointment. The dating to 407 BCE is secure, though some sources place it in 406 BCE. The consensus favors 407 BCE.

events.title: Battle of Notium
events.description: Alcibiades' fleet was defeated by Lysander's Spartan fleet near Notium in Ionia. This was a significant setback for Athens and a demonstration of Lysander's superior naval tactics and leadership. The defeat undermined Alcibiades' political position in Athens and contributed to his eventual exile. The battle showed that Spartan naval power, backed by Persian gold, was now competitive with Athens' traditional naval superiority.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon provides a detailed account of the battle. The dating to 407 BCE is secure.

events.title: Alcibiades exiled again from Athens
events.description: Following the defeat at Notium, Alcibiades was again exiled from Athens. The Athenian assembly, frustrated by his military setback and his controversial political history, voted to remove him from command and exile him. Alcibiades fled to Thrace and later to Phrygia, where he lived in exile until his death. His second exile marked the end of his political influence in Athens and removed one of the few figures who might have provided effective military leadership in the final years of the war.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Alcibiades' exile. The dating to 407 BCE is secure.

events.title: Carthaginian campaign in Sicily continues
events.description: Hannibal's Carthaginian forces continued their military campaigns in Sicily, capturing additional Greek cities and consolidating Carthaginian control over the western and central parts of the island. The campaigns were marked by brutal sieges and the destruction of Greek cities. The ongoing Carthaginian threat destabilized Greek Sicily and contributed to the political fragmentation that would eventually lead to Dionysius I's rise to power.
events.certainty_note: Diodorus provides accounts of Carthaginian campaigns in Sicily during this period, but specific events for 407 BCE are not always clearly dated. The general context of ongoing Carthaginian expansion is secure.

events.title: Cyrus the Younger appointed satrap of Lydia
events.description: Cyrus the Younger, son of Artaxerxes II, was appointed satrap of Lydia and other territories in western Asia Minor. This appointment gave him significant power and resources, which he would later use to support Lysander and the Spartan fleet against Athens. Cyrus' position as satrap made him a key player in Persian-Greek relations during the final years of the Peloponnesian War. His later rebellion (401 BCE) would have major consequences for the Persian Empire and Greece.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon mentions Cyrus' position as satrap, but the exact date of his appointment is not clearly specified. The traditional dating places it around 407 BCE, but some sources suggest 408 or 406 BCE. The year 407 BCE is conventional but not definitively confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Alcibiades' return to Athens is sometimes placed in 408 BCE in alternative chronologies. The Battle of Notium is occasionally dated to 406 BCE. Lysander's appointment as admiral is sometimes dated to 406 BCE rather than 407 BCE. These variations reflect the challenges of precise dating in ancient history, particularly when sources are not always consistent. The exact date of Cyrus the Younger's appointment as satrap is uncertain and may have occurred in 408 or 406 BCE rather than 407 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 407 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides a near-contemporary account of the Peloponnesian War. Plutarch's biographies of Alcibiades and Lysander provide additional details but were written centuries later. Sicilian history relies on Diodorus Siculus, who wrote much later. Persian history is less well-documented for this specific year, relying on Greek accounts and limited Persian sources. Chinese history depends on much later compilations. The bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the record is particularly focused on military and political events in the Aegean region.

era_context: The Peloponnesian War continues in its final decade. Athens faces mounting military and economic pressure, while Sparta, with Persian financial backing, strengthens its position. In Sicily, the Carthaginian invasion continues to advance against Greek cities. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains stable and increasingly influential in Greek affairs. In China, the Warring States period continues with ongoing interstate conflicts.

events.title: Athenian naval operations and grain supply crisis
events.region: Athens, Aegean Sea, Black Sea region
events.description: Athens faced a critical grain supply crisis as Spartan naval operations and Persian-backed forces disrupted trade routes from the Black Sea, Athens' primary source of imported grain. The Athenian fleet conducted operations to protect grain convoys and maintain control of key straits, particularly the Hellespont. This economic pressure was one of the most significant factors in Athens' eventual defeat, as the city could not sustain its population without imported grain. The crisis forced Athens to divert military resources to protect commerce rather than conduct offensive operations.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War 2.38

events.certainty_note: The grain supply crisis is well-documented in general terms, but specific events for 408 BCE are not always clearly dated. The crisis was ongoing throughout the final years of the war.

events.title: Spartan naval expansion with Persian support
events.description: Sparta, with financial support from Persia (particularly from Cyrus the Younger), expanded its naval fleet and operations. This represented a dramatic shift in the balance of power, as Sparta had traditionally been a land-based power while Athens dominated the seas. The influx of Persian gold allowed Sparta to build and maintain a competitive fleet, which was crucial to its eventual victory in the Peloponnesian War. This period saw the beginning of Spartan naval supremacy that would last for several decades.
key_figures: Cyrus the Younger (Persian satrap, financial supporter)
events.certainty_note: The general trend of Spartan naval expansion with Persian support is well-documented, but specific events for 408 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Carthaginian siege of Himera
events.description: Hannibal's Carthaginian forces besieged and captured the Greek city of Himera in northern Sicily. The siege was brutal, resulting in the destruction of the city and the death or enslavement of thousands of inhabitants. The fall of Himera was a major Carthaginian victory and further consolidated Carthaginian control over Sicily. The destruction of Himera was particularly significant because it was one of the oldest and most important Greek colonies in Sicily, and its fall symbolized the decline of Greek power on the island.
events.certainty_note: Diodorus provides a detailed account of the siege of Himera. The dating to 408 BCE is secure in the historical tradition.

events.title: Athenian democracy under strain
events.description: Athens' democratic system was under severe strain due to the prolonged war, economic crisis, and military defeats. The assembly was increasingly divided over war strategy, and there were growing calls for peace negotiations. The execution of the generals after Arginusae (406 BCE) had demonstrated the dangers of mob justice and weakened public confidence in democratic institutions. Political factions competed for influence, and the city's leadership was increasingly unstable. This period of internal discord would eventually lead to the oligarchic coup of 404 BCE.

sources.name: Aristophanes, Frogs (produced 405 BCE, reflects contemporary concerns)

events.certainty_note: The general context of political strain in Athens is well-documented, but specific events for 408 BCE are not always clearly dated. This entry reflects the broader historical trajectory.

events.title: Artaxerxes II consolidates Persian control
events.description: Artaxerxes II continued to rule the Persian Empire, consolidating his control and managing the empire's vast territories. His reign was marked by relative stability, though he faced periodic challenges to his authority. The Persian Empire's intervention in Greek affairs, particularly through financial support for Sparta, was a significant factor in the outcome of the Peloponnesian War. Artaxerxes' decision to support Sparta over Athens was motivated by Persian strategic interests in maintaining a balance of power in Greece.
events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for 408 BCE in Persian history. This entry describes the broader political context.

events.title: Warring States period: Continued interstate conflicts in China
events.description: The Warring States period in China continued with ongoing military campaigns and diplomatic maneuvering among the various states. The state of Chu was expanding its power in central and southern China, while states like Qi, Yan, and others competed for dominance. This era saw the development of advanced military tactics, the rise of professional armies, and the consolidation of state power. The period was also marked by significant cultural and intellectual developments, including the flourishing of philosophical schools.
key_figures: Various rulers of Chu, Qi, Yan, and other states (specific identities uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are often compiled centuries after events. Specific events for 408 BCE are not clearly dated in surviving sources.

The specific events of 408 BCE in Athens are not always clearly distinguished from those of 407 or 409 BCE in the sources. The dating of Cyrus the Younger's appointment as satrap is uncertain and may have occurred in 407 or 406 BCE rather than 408 BCE. These variations reflect the challenges of precise dating in ancient history.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 408 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides a near-contemporary account of the Peloponnesian War. However, specific events for 408 BCE are often not clearly dated, and the sources tend to group events by year or season rather than providing precise chronology. Sicilian history relies on Diodorus Siculus, who wrote much later. Persian history is less well-documented for this specific year. Chinese history depends on much later compilations. The bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the record is particularly focused on military and political events in the Aegean region.

era_context: The Peloponnesian War continues in its final decade. Athens, despite its traditional naval superiority, faces mounting pressure from Sparta and Persian-backed forces. In Sicily, the Carthaginian invasion under Hannibal begins, marking a major shift in the balance of power on the island. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II remains stable and increasingly influential in Greek affairs. In China, the Warring States period continues with ongoing interstate conflicts and the consolidation of state power.

events.title: Carthaginian invasion of Sicily under Hannibal
events.description: Hannibal, grandson of Hamilcar Barca, led a major Carthaginian invasion of Sicily, beginning a campaign that would last several years and fundamentally alter the balance of power on the island. The invasion was ostensibly in response to Carthaginian grievances against Greek cities, but it was primarily motivated by Carthage's desire to expand its control over Sicily and its resources. The campaign was marked by brutal sieges, mass casualties, and the destruction of major Greek cities. The invasion destabilized Greek Sicily and contributed to the political fragmentation that would eventually lead to Dionysius I's rise to power.
events.certainty_note: Diodorus provides detailed accounts of the invasion's beginning. The dating to 409 BCE is secure in the historical tradition, though some sources place the beginning in 410 BCE.

events.title: Carthaginian siege of Selinus
events.description: Hannibal's Carthaginian forces besieged and captured the Greek city of Selinus in southwestern Sicily. The siege was brutal and resulted in the destruction of much of the city and the death or enslavement of thousands of inhabitants. The fall of Selinus was one of the first major Carthaginian victories in the invasion and demonstrated the vulnerability of Greek cities to Carthaginian military power. The destruction of Selinus was particularly significant because it was one of the oldest and most important Greek colonies in Sicily.
key_figures: Hermocrates (Syracusan general, attempting to relieve Selinus)
events.certainty_note: Diodorus provides a detailed account of the siege of Selinus. The dating to 409 BCE is secure in the historical tradition.

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Aegean
events.description: Athens continued to conduct naval operations in the Aegean Sea, attempting to maintain control of key islands and straits and to protect its trade routes. The Athenian fleet, though still superior to Spartan forces at this time, was increasingly stretched thin by the need to defend multiple theaters of operation. The naval operations were costly in terms of resources and manpower, contributing to Athens' economic strain. This period saw ongoing skirmishes and naval engagements between Athenian and Spartan forces.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Athenian naval activities during this period, but specific operations for 409 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Spartan military operations in the Peloponnese
events.description: Sparta continued to conduct military operations against Athens and its allies in the Peloponnese. The Spartan army, under the command of various generals, maintained pressure on Athenian-allied cities and territories. The Spartan strategy was to wear down Athens through a combination of military pressure, economic blockade, and diplomatic isolation. This period saw ongoing campaigns and skirmishes in the Peloponnese and central Greece.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon documents Spartan military activities during this period, but specific operations for 409 BCE are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II
events.description: Artaxerxes II continued to rule the Persian Empire, consolidating his control and managing the empire's vast territories. His reign was marked by relative stability and the consolidation of Persian power. The Persian Empire remained the dominant power in the Near East, with influence extending from Egypt to Central Asia. Persian gold and diplomacy played an increasingly important role in Greek affairs, particularly in supporting Sparta against Athens. This period saw the beginning of Persian intervention in Greek politics that would shape the outcome of the Peloponnesian War.
events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for 409 BCE in Persian history. This entry describes the broader political context.

events.title: Warring States period: Interstate conflicts in China
events.description: The Warring States period in China continued with ongoing military campaigns and diplomatic maneuvering among the various states. The state of Chu was expanding its power in central and southern China, while states like Qi, Yan, and others competed for dominance. This era saw the development of advanced military tactics, the rise of professional armies, and the consolidation of state power. The period was also marked by significant cultural and intellectual developments, including the flourishing of philosophical schools such as Confucianism, Daoism, and Legalism.
key_figures: Various rulers of Chu, Qi, Yan, and other states (specific identities uncertain for this year)

The dating of the siege of Selinus is sometimes placed in 408 BCE. These variations reflect the challenges of precise dating in ancient history, particularly when sources are not always consistent. Diodorus Siculus, the primary source for Sicilian history during this period, wrote centuries after the events and may have had access to sources that are no longer extant.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 409 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica for the Peloponnesian War and Diodorus Siculus for Sicilian history. However, specific events for 409 BCE are often not clearly dated, and the sources tend to group events by year or season rather than providing precise chronology. Diodorus is the primary source for the Carthaginian invasion of Sicily, but he wrote much later and may have had access to earlier sources that are no longer extant. Persian history is less well-documented for this specific year. Chinese history depends on much later compilations. The bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the record is particularly focused on military and political events in the Aegean region and Sicily.

era_context: The Classical Greek world during the final decade of the Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE). Athens, weakened by the Sicilian Expedition (415–413 BCE), faces internal political instability and external military pressure from Sparta and its allies. The Persian Empire remains a distant but influential power in eastern Mediterranean affairs. This period marks the beginning of the end of Athenian democratic hegemony and the rise of Spartan dominance in Greece.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (contemporary Chinese records exist but are not well-integrated into Western historical synthesis)

events.title: Oligarchic coup in Athens; establishment of the Four Hundred
events.description: A radical oligarchic faction, led by Antiphon, Phrynichus, and Pisander, orchestrated the overthrow of Athenian democracy. The Four Hundred, a council of oligarchs, seized power with the promise of securing Persian financial support for the war effort. The coup was enabled by the absence of the Athenian fleet at Samos and widespread war fatigue. The oligarchy lasted approximately four months before being overthrown by democratic forces, but it represented a profound constitutional crisis and the first successful suspension of Athenian democracy.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides was present in Athens during this period and provides detailed contemporary narrative. The event is also corroborated by later sources.

events.title: Democratic restoration in Athens; fall of the Four Hundred
events.description: After approximately four months of oligarchic rule, democratic forces led by Thrasybulus and supported by the Athenian fleet at Samos orchestrated the restoration of democracy. The Four Hundred were deposed, and a more moderate oligarchy (the Five Thousand) briefly held power before full democracy was restored. This event demonstrated the resilience of Athenian democratic institutions and the power of the navy as a political force independent of the city proper.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Contemporary documentation by Thucydides and corroborated by later sources.
cross_references: Part of broader democratic restoration process in -410-003

events.title: Battle of Cynossema; Athenian naval victory in the Hellespont
events.description: The Athenian fleet, under the command of Thrasybulus and Alcibiades, defeated a Spartan-Corinthian fleet in the Hellespont. This was the first significant Athenian naval victory after the disaster at Syracuse (413 BCE) and provided crucial morale boost to the democratic restoration movement. The victory secured Athenian control of grain supply routes from the Black Sea, vital to the city's survival. The battle demonstrated that Athenian naval superiority, though damaged, was not permanently lost.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides provides detailed contemporary account. The strategic significance is corroborated by subsequent historical developments.
cross_references: Part of broader Peloponnesian War conflict

events.title: Spartan-Persian negotiations regarding naval support
events.description: Following the Athenian oligarchic coup and its failure, Sparta intensified negotiations with the Persian satrap Tissaphernes for financial support to build a fleet capable of challenging Athenian naval dominance. These negotiations, which had begun earlier in the war, became more concrete in 410 BCE. The Persian Empire sought to prevent either Greek power from achieving hegemony while extracting concessions regarding Ionian Greek cities. This diplomatic maneuvering would shape the final years of the Peloponnesian War.
key_figures: Tissaphernes (Persian satrap of Lydia and Caria)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents these negotiations, though the precise timing and details of individual diplomatic exchanges are sometimes unclear. The general trajectory is well-established.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian involvement in Peloponnesian War

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative emphasizes the Four Hundred as a unified oligarchic faction, but modern scholarship (particularly following the work of David Stockton and others) has noted significant internal divisions within the oligarchy between radical oligarchs (like Antiphon) and more moderate figures (like Theramenes). The coup was not as monolithic or as ideologically coherent as earlier accounts suggested. Additionally, the extent to which Persian support was promised or actually delivered remains debated; Thucydides' account may overstate the coherence of Persian strategy. The dating of the oligarchy's fall to precisely four months is traditional but not independently verified.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 410 BCE is exceptionally rich due to Thucydides' contemporary narrative, which ends in this year. However, Thucydides' account is not without bias: he was an Athenian and may have emphasized democratic restoration more favorably than Spartan sources would. The oligarchic coup is also documented in Aristotle's Constitution of the Athenians, written a century later but drawing on earlier sources now lost. The perspective of Sparta, Corinth, and Persia is less directly represented. Archaeological evidence from this period is limited, though numismatic evidence (coins) can illuminate economic conditions.

era_context: The Peloponnesian War enters its twentieth year. Athens, reeling from the catastrophic Sicilian Expedition (415–413 BCE), faces simultaneous threats from Sparta, its allies, and internal political instability. The oligarchic conspiracy that would culminate in the coup of 410 BCE is already in motion. Sparta, with increasing Persian financial support, is building a navy to challenge Athenian dominance. This is a year of profound crisis for the Athenian state.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (contemporary Chinese records exist but are not integrated)

events.title: Oligarchic conspiracy in Athens; planning of the Four Hundred coup
events.description: A secret oligarchic faction, including Antiphon, Phrynichus, Pisander, and others, began organizing a conspiracy to overthrow Athenian democracy. The conspirators exploited war fatigue, economic hardship, and the absence of the Athenian fleet (stationed at Samos) to plan a coup. They claimed that oligarchic rule would secure Persian financial support from Tissaphernes, though this promise was likely exaggerated or false. The conspiracy operated in secret societies (hetaireiai) and used intimidation against potential opponents. The coup would be executed in 410 BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides provides detailed account of the conspiracy's origins and organization. The dating to 411 BCE is based on Thucydides' narrative structure.

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Hellespont and Aegean
events.description: The Athenian fleet, under commanders including Thrasybulus, conducted operations to maintain control of the Hellespont and secure grain supply routes from the Black Sea. These operations were crucial to Athens' survival, as the city depended on imported grain. The fleet also attempted to prevent Spartan-Corinthian naval expansion in the region. These campaigns were part of the broader strategy to maintain Athenian power despite the loss of resources and manpower from the Sicilian disaster.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents these operations, though specific dates and details of individual engagements are sometimes unclear. The general pattern is well-established.
cross_references: Part of broader Peloponnesian War naval conflict

events.title: Spartan-Persian negotiations intensify; Lysander's rise
events.description: Sparta intensified diplomatic efforts with the Persian satrap Tissaphernes to secure financial support for naval construction. These negotiations were part of a broader Persian strategy to prevent Athenian hegemony while maintaining influence over Ionian Greek cities. The negotiations would eventually lead to the appointment of Lysander as Spartan commander, who would prove far more effective at securing Persian support than previous commanders. The Persian Empire's involvement was crucial to Sparta's ability to challenge Athenian naval dominance.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents these negotiations, though the precise timing and details are sometimes unclear. Lysander's rise to prominence is better documented in later sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Persian involvement in Peloponnesian War

events.title: Alcibiades in exile; diplomatic contacts with Sparta and Persia
events.description: Alcibiades, the brilliant but controversial Athenian general, remained in exile following his recall from Sicily in 415 BCE. He maintained diplomatic contacts with both Sparta and Persia, offering his services and counsel to whichever power would benefit him most. Alcibiades was consulted by the oligarchic conspirators in Athens regarding Persian support, though his actual influence on events is debated. His presence as a potential alternative leader complicated Athenian politics and demonstrated the fragility of democratic institutions.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents Alcibiades' diplomatic activities. The extent of his influence on the oligarchic conspiracy is debated by modern scholars.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative presents the oligarchic conspiracy as a unified, ideologically coherent movement, but modern scholarship has questioned this interpretation. The conspirators had diverse motivations: some were genuinely oligarchic in ideology, while others (like Theramenes) were more moderate and may have been coerced into participation. The promise of Persian support, which was central to the conspirators' pitch, is now viewed with skepticism by many scholars; Tissaphernes may have deliberately misled the conspirators. Additionally, the extent of Alcibiades' influence on the conspiracy is disputed; Thucydides may have overstated his role. Some modern scholars argue that the conspiracy was less a coherent political movement and more a series of opportunistic power grabs by competing factions.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 411 BCE is rich due to Thucydides' contemporary narrative, which covers this year in detail. However, Thucydides' account ends shortly after this year, and subsequent events are known primarily through later sources like Xenophon's Hellenica and Aristotle's Constitution of the Athenians. The perspective of Sparta, Corinth, and Persia is less directly represented. Thucydides' account may be biased toward the democratic perspective, though he attempts to present multiple viewpoints. The oligarchic conspirators left no contemporary written records, so their motivations and organization are known primarily through hostile sources.
