era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period, dominated by the aftermath of the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens under Cimon and the early Delian League; Sparta consolidating Peloponnesian hegemony. Egypt under Persian rule (Achaemenid Empire); China in the Spring and Autumn period transitioning toward the Warring States era. This is the age of early Greek philosophy, temple construction, and the consolidation of democratic institutions in Athens.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (sparse contemporary records)
Mesoamerica (no written records in this period)

events.title: Pericles rises to prominence in Athens; democratic reforms accelerate
events.description: Pericles, backed by popular support and the ostracism of his rival Cimon (461 BCE), emerges as the leading statesman of Athens. He champions radical democratic reforms, including payment for jury service and expansion of the citizen assembly's powers. This marks the beginning of the Periclean Age, which will define Athenian cultural and political dominance for the next three decades. The shift consolidates power in the hands of the demos (common people) rather than the aristocracy.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (retrospective account)

events.certainty_note: Pericles' rise is well-attested in later sources, but precise dating of his ascendancy varies (461–460 BCE range). Cimon's ostracism is dated to 461 BCE by most sources; Pericles' prominence solidifies in 460 BCE.

events.title: Egyptian revolt against Persian rule; Inarus leads uprising
events.description: Inarus, a Libyan prince, leads a major rebellion against Achaemenid Persian rule in Egypt. He gains control of much of the Delta and Lower Egypt, declaring himself pharaoh. The revolt is initially successful, with Persian forces under Artaxerxes I struggling to suppress it. Inarus seeks alliance with Athens, which sends a naval expedition to support the rebellion. This conflict will dominate Egyptian politics for the next several years and represents a significant challenge to Persian imperial authority.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The rebellion is well-attested in Greek sources, though Egyptian records are sparse. Dating to 460 BCE is conventional; some sources place the outbreak in 461 BCE. The chronology of the revolt is debated among modern scholars.

events.title: Construction of the Temple of Zeus at Olympia begins (or continues)
events.description: The Temple of Zeus at Olympia, one of the greatest Doric temples of Classical Greece, is under construction or in advanced stages of completion. Designed by the architect Libon of Elis, the temple will house the famous chryselephantine (gold and ivory) statue of Zeus by Phidias. The temple represents the height of Classical Greek architectural achievement and religious devotion. Its construction reflects the prosperity and cultural confidence of the Greek city-states in the post-Persian War period.
events.certainty_note: Construction dates for the Temple of Zeus are debated. Most scholars place the main construction phase between 470–456 BCE, with completion around 456 BCE. The temple may have been under construction in 460 BCE, but the exact year is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Pericles' rise to power is sometimes placed at 461 BCE rather than 460 BCE, depending on the source. The Egyptian revolt's exact start date is disputed: some sources suggest 461 BCE. The Temple of Zeus construction chronology is debated among archaeologists; some place the primary building phase slightly later (465–456 BCE). No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of these events, but precision in dating should be treated with caution.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 460 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Athenian sources, which creates a Western-centric bias. Egyptian and Persian perspectives are largely lost or filtered through Greek accounts. The Athenian sources (Thucydides, Plutarch) are retrospective, written decades or centuries later, and reflect Athenian political interests. Archaeological evidence from Olympia and Athens provides material corroboration but does not resolve all dating ambiguities. The record is richest for Athens and Greece; sparser for Egypt, Persia, and other regions.

graph_edges.note: Pericles' rise follows directly from Cimon's ostracism in 461 BCE
Egyptian revolt prompts Athenian military intervention
Both reflect the cultural and political confidence of Classical Athens

era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period, immediately following the Persian Wars. Athens is consolidating its naval power and democratic institutions. Sparta remains the dominant land power in Greece. The Delian League, nominally a defensive alliance against Persia, is increasingly dominated by Athens. China is in the Spring and Autumn period; Egypt is under Achaemenid Persian rule. This is a pivotal year for Athenian political transformation.

events.title: Cimon ostracized from Athens; democratic faction gains power
events.description: Cimon, the conservative aristocratic leader and military hero of the Persian Wars, is ostracized from Athens through a popular vote. His ostracism reflects a power struggle between the conservative faction (favoring alliance with Sparta and limited democracy) and the radical democratic faction (favoring expansion of the Delian League and democratic reforms). Ephialtes, the democratic reformer, leads the opposition to Cimon. This ostracism marks a decisive shift in Athenian politics toward radical democracy and imperial expansion. Cimon will spend ten years in exile.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War

events.certainty_note: Cimon's ostracism in 461 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader democratic transformation of Athens

events.title: Ephialtes assassinated; democratic reforms continue
events.description: Ephialtes, the leading democratic reformer who orchestrated Cimon's ostracism, is assassinated by political opponents. The murder is attributed to conservative aristocrats who oppose his radical democratic agenda. Despite his death, Ephialtes' reforms—particularly the curtailment of the Areopagus (the aristocratic council) and the expansion of democratic institutions—are carried forward by his political allies, especially Pericles. His assassination marks the intensity of political conflict in Athens but does not reverse the democratic tide.
events.certainty_note: Ephialtes' assassination is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Most sources place it in 461 BCE, shortly after Cimon's ostracism, but some suggest 462 BCE. The identity of the assassins is not definitively known.

events.title: First Peloponnesian War begins; Athens and Sparta enter conflict
events.description: Tensions between Athens and Sparta escalate into open conflict. The immediate cause is disputed: some sources cite Athenian support for Megara (a Corinthian ally) against Corinth; others point to broader rivalry over hegemony in Greece. The conflict is characterized by raids, naval engagements, and proxy wars. Unlike the later Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE), this conflict is intermittent and less devastating, but it marks the beginning of a 30-year period of Athenian-Spartan rivalry. The war reflects the incompatibility of Athenian naval imperialism and Spartan land-based hegemony.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the First Peloponnesian War in 461 BCE is well-attested in ancient sources. Thucydides provides a detailed account of the causes and early phases.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern leading to -431-001 (Second Peloponnesian War)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Ephialtes' assassination is disputed; some sources place it in 462 BCE rather than 461 BCE. The causes of the First Peloponnesian War are debated: Thucydides emphasizes Corinthian grievances, but modern scholars debate the role of Athenian imperial ambitions versus Spartan fear. The significance of the war itself is sometimes downplayed in modern scholarship, which views it as a prelude to the more famous Peloponnesian War (431–404 BCE) rather than as a major conflict in its own right.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 461 BCE is dominated by Athenian sources, particularly Thucydides and Plutarch, both writing decades or centuries after the events. Spartan and Corinthian perspectives are largely absent or filtered through Athenian accounts. The sources reflect Athenian political interests and may overstate Athenian agency. Archaeological evidence is limited for this year. The record is richest for Athens and Greece; sparse for other regions.

graph_edges.note: Cimon's ostracism intensifies political conflict, contributing to Ephialtes' assassination
Ephialtes' death clears the way for Pericles' political dominance
Cimon's ostracism and the outbreak of war reflect the same political realignment
First Peloponnesian War establishes pattern of Athenian-Spartan rivalry leading to the Second Peloponnesian War

era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period, in the immediate aftermath of the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens is consolidating its naval power and beginning to transform from a defensive alliance (Delian League) into an imperial power. Sparta remains the dominant land power. Egypt is under Achaemenid Persian rule. China is in the Spring and Autumn period. This is a transitional year in Athenian political development, with democratic reforms accelerating.

events.title: Ephialtes leads democratic reforms; Areopagus powers curtailed
events.description: Ephialtes, a radical democratic reformer, leads a series of constitutional reforms that dramatically reduce the power of the Areopagus, the ancient aristocratic council that had dominated Athenian politics. The reforms transfer judicial and legislative powers to the citizen assembly (ekklesia) and the popular courts (dikasteria). This marks a decisive shift toward radical democracy, where power rests with the common people rather than the aristocracy. The reforms are controversial and provoke fierce opposition from conservative forces, setting the stage for Ephialtes' assassination in 461 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The democratic reforms are well-attested, but their precise dating is debated. Most scholars place them in 462 BCE, but some suggest 461 BCE. The reforms may have been implemented gradually over several years.
cross_references: Part of broader democratic transformation of Athens

events.title: Athenian expedition to Egypt; support for Inarus rebellion
events.description: Athens sends a naval expedition to Egypt to support the rebellion of Inarus against Achaemenid Persian rule. The expedition is part of Athens' broader strategy to challenge Persian power and expand its influence in the eastern Mediterranean. The Athenian fleet, numbering around 200 ships according to some sources, arrives in Egypt and joins forces with Inarus. This intervention marks a significant escalation of Athenian imperial ambitions and represents an attempt to weaken Persian control over Egypt. The expedition will ultimately fail, but it demonstrates Athens' willingness to project power far from home.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The Athenian expedition to Egypt is attested in Greek sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Some sources place it in 462 BCE, others in 460 BCE. The chronology of the Egyptian rebellion and Athenian involvement is debated among modern scholars.
cross_references: Leads to -456-001 (Athenian forces defeated in Egypt)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ephialtes' reforms is disputed; some sources place them in 461 BCE rather than 462 BCE. The Athenian expedition to Egypt may have begun in 460 BCE rather than 462 BCE, depending on the source. Modern scholarship debates the extent and significance of Ephialtes' reforms; some scholars argue that the Areopagus retained more power than ancient sources suggest.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 462 BCE is sparse and dominated by later Greek sources, particularly Aristotle and Plutarch, who wrote centuries after the events. Contemporary sources are lacking. The sources reflect Athenian political interests and may distort the historical record. Archaeological evidence is limited. The record is richest for Athens; sparse for Egypt, Persia, and other regions.

graph_edges.note: Ephialtes' democratic reforms provoke conservative opposition, leading to his assassination
Athenian support for Egyptian rebellion against Persia

era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period, approximately 15–20 years after the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens is consolidating its naval power and beginning to transform the Delian League into an Athenian empire. Sparta remains the dominant land power in Greece. Egypt is under Achaemenid Persian rule. China is in the Spring and Autumn period, with the state of Lu and other kingdoms competing for dominance. This is a period of relative stability in Greece before the outbreak of the First Peloponnesian War.

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Aegean; consolidation of Delian League
events.description: Athens conducts naval operations throughout the Aegean to consolidate control over the Delian League, the alliance of Greek city-states nominally formed to defend against Persian aggression. Athens increasingly uses the league's resources for its own purposes, transforming it from a defensive alliance into an instrument of Athenian imperial power. These operations include the suppression of rebellious allies and the collection of tribute. The consolidation of the Delian League is a key step in the development of the Athenian Empire and contributes to growing tensions with Sparta.
key_figures: Cimon (if recalled from ostracism, though this is uncertain)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: Athenian naval operations in the Aegean during this period are attested in general terms, but specific events in 463 BCE are not well-documented. The date is assigned by convention to represent ongoing consolidation of the Delian League.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of Athenian imperial expansion

disconfirming_evidence: No specific major events are well-attested for 463 BCE. The year may have been relatively quiet in terms of documented historical events. Modern scholarship emphasizes the gradual nature of Athenian imperial consolidation rather than dramatic events in any single year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 463 BCE is very sparse. No major events are specifically documented for this year in surviving sources. The year is largely a gap in the historical record, filled by inference from broader patterns of Athenian naval activity and Delian League consolidation. The sources that do exist are retrospective accounts written centuries later. Archaeological evidence is limited. This year represents a significant gap in our knowledge of Greek history.

graph_edges.note: Athenian consolidation of the Delian League contributes to tensions with Sparta, leading to the First Peloponnesian War

era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period, approximately 15–20 years after the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens is consolidating its naval power; Sparta is the dominant land power in Greece. Egypt is under Achaemenid Persian rule. China is in the Spring and Autumn period. This is a period of relative stability in Greece, though tensions between Athens and Sparta are building. The year 464 BCE is notable for a major earthquake in Sparta and the subsequent helot rebellion.

events.title: Great earthquake in Sparta; massive destruction and casualties
events.description: A catastrophic earthquake strikes Sparta, causing massive destruction of buildings and infrastructure and killing thousands of people (estimates range from 20,000 to 50,000 deaths, though these figures are likely exaggerated in ancient sources). The earthquake is one of the most severe natural disasters recorded in ancient Greece. The destruction is so severe that it destabilizes Spartan society and military power. The earthquake triggers a major helot (serf) rebellion, as the enslaved population of Laconia and Messenia sees an opportunity to revolt against Spartan rule. The disaster marks a turning point in Spartan history and has significant geopolitical consequences.
key_figures: Spartan kings and ephors (responding to crisis)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The earthquake in Sparta in 464 BCE is well-attested in multiple ancient sources. The date is secure, though the death toll estimates vary widely and are likely exaggerated.
cross_references: Leads to -463-001 (Athenian response to Spartan crisis)

events.title: Helot rebellion in Laconia and Messenia; Spartan crisis
events.description: Following the earthquake, the helots (enslaved population) of Laconia and Messenia rise in rebellion against Spartan rule. The rebellion is widespread and well-organized, with helots seizing the opportunity to challenge Spartan dominance. The rebels fortify themselves in strongholds, particularly Mount Ithome in Messenia, and resist Spartan military efforts to suppress them. The rebellion lasts for several years and severely strains Spartan military resources. Sparta is forced to seek help from its allies, including Athens, which sends a force under Cimon to assist in suppressing the rebellion. The helot rebellion is a major crisis for Sparta and demonstrates the fragility of Spartan power despite its military reputation.
key_figures: Spartan kings and ephors (responding to crisis)
Cimon (Athenian commander, sent to assist Sparta)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1

events.certainty_note: The helot rebellion following the earthquake is well-attested in ancient sources. The rebellion began in 464 BCE and lasted until approximately 455 BCE. The date of the outbreak is secure.
cross_references: Leads to -462-001 (Athenian assistance to Sparta under Cimon)

disconfirming_evidence: The death toll from the earthquake is reported in ancient sources as extremely high (20,000–50,000), but modern scholars consider these figures exaggerated. The exact magnitude and extent of the earthquake are debated among modern seismologists and historians. The helot rebellion is sometimes dated to begin in 465 BCE rather than 464 BCE, depending on the source. The rebellion's duration and intensity are debated; some sources suggest it was less organized than others imply.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 464 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Thucydides and Plutarch, both writing decades or centuries after the events. The sources reflect Athenian and Greek perspectives; Spartan and helot perspectives are largely absent. The death toll estimates are likely exaggerated for dramatic effect. Archaeological evidence from Sparta may corroborate the earthquake but does not resolve all dating ambiguities. The record is richest for Sparta and Greece; sparse for other regions.

graph_edges.note: The earthquake directly triggers the helot rebellion
The helot rebellion prompts Sparta to seek Athenian assistance under Cimon
The earthquake and helot rebellion weaken Sparta, contributing to the outbreak of the First Peloponnesian War

era_context: The Classical Greek world during the early decades following the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens is consolidating its naval power and beginning its rise to hegemony in the Aegean under the leadership of figures like Cimon and later Pericles. The Delian League, ostensibly a defensive alliance against Persian aggression, is transforming into an Athenian empire. Simultaneously, Sparta maintains land dominance in the Peloponnese, and tensions between these two powers are mounting. In the East, the Achaemenid Persian Empire under Artaxerxes I is stabilizing after internal succession struggles.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman contact zones
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited sources for this period)
East Asia beyond fragmentary Chinese records

events.title: Revolt of the Egyptian satrap Inarus against Persian rule
events.description: Inarus, a Libyan prince, led a major rebellion against Achaemenid Persian control of Egypt, with support from Athens. The revolt was part of a broader pattern of resistance to Persian imperial authority in the eastern Mediterranean. Athenian naval forces under Charitimides were dispatched to support the rebellion, marking an early instance of Athenian intervention in Persian-controlled territories. The rebellion was ultimately suppressed by Persian forces under Megabyzus around 454 BCE, but the initial uprising in 465 BCE represents a significant moment of anti-Persian mobilization.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The rebellion is well-attested in Greek sources, but the precise dating of its outbreak is uncertain. Thucydides places it in the context of the 460s BCE, but exact chronology depends on reconstruction of the Athenian archon list and Egyptian chronology.

events.title: Ostracism of Cimon at Athens
events.description: Cimon, the prominent Athenian general and statesman who had led successful campaigns against the Persians and expanded Athenian naval power, was ostracized (exiled for ten years) by popular vote. This removal from power marked a significant shift in Athenian politics, as it reflected growing opposition to his pro-Spartan foreign policy and his dominance in Athenian affairs. The ostracism paved the way for the rise of more radical democratic figures and those favoring an anti-Spartan stance. The precise date of Cimon's ostracism is debated among scholars, with some placing it in 461 BCE, but 465 BCE is also proposed by some ancient sources.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War

events.certainty_note: The date of Cimon's ostracism is one of the most disputed chronological questions in Classical Athenian history. Ancient sources give conflicting dates (461 vs. 465 BCE). Modern scholarship is divided, with some favoring 461 BCE based on the archon list reconstruction.
cross_references: Led to rise of Pericles in subsequent years

events.title: Earthquake and helot revolt in Sparta
events.description: A major earthquake struck the Spartan region, causing significant destruction and loss of life. The disaster triggered or exacerbated a revolt among the helots (enslaved population) of Sparta, who saw the catastrophe as an opportunity to rebel against their masters. The helot uprising, combined with the earthquake's devastation, severely weakened Spartan military capacity and forced the state to seek external assistance. This event had major geopolitical consequences, as it temporarily reduced Spartan power and altered the balance of forces in Greece.
key_figures: Archidamus II (Spartan king during the crisis)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The earthquake is well-attested in ancient sources, and geological evidence supports major seismic activity in the Spartan region during the mid-5th century BCE. However, the precise year is uncertain; sources variously place it between 465–464 BCE.
cross_references: Led to -464-001 (helot revolt continuation and Athenian intervention)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Cimon's ostracism remains highly contested. While traditionally placed in 465 BCE by some scholars, the majority of modern scholarship now favors 461 BCE based on more rigorous reconstruction of the Athenian archon list. The earthquake and helot revolt are well-attested, but the precise year (465 vs. 464 BCE) is debated. Some sources suggest the earthquake occurred in 464 BCE, making the dating of the initial seismic event uncertain by approximately one year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 465 BCE is dominated by Athenian and Greek sources, particularly Thucydides and Plutarch, both writing decades or centuries after the events. There is a significant Western bias in surviving documentation. Egyptian and Persian perspectives on the Inarus rebellion are largely lost, known only through Greek accounts. The dating of events in this period depends heavily on the reconstruction of the Athenian archon list, which itself contains uncertainties. Archaeological evidence for the earthquake is more reliable than textual dating.

graph_edges.note: The Inarus rebellion was eventually suppressed by Persian forces under Megabyzus
The earthquake triggered or exacerbated the helot revolt that continued into 464 BCE
Cimon's ostracism may have occurred in 461 BCE rather than 465 BCE; the events are related to the same political realignment

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-5th century BCE, during the consolidation phase following the Persian Wars. Athens is expanding its naval dominance through the Delian League, while Sparta remains the dominant land power. The period is marked by increasing tensions between these two powers and their respective allies. In the Persian Empire, Artaxerxes I is consolidating his rule after the succession crisis following Xerxes' death. The broader Mediterranean is experiencing a shift from Persian hegemony toward Greek independence and inter-Greek competition.
geographic_coverage_gaps: India (limited sources for this specific year)

events.title: Athenian naval campaign against Persian-held territories
events.description: Athens, under the leadership of Cimon, conducted naval operations against Persian-controlled territories in the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean. These campaigns were part of the broader Athenian strategy to expand the Delian League and reduce Persian influence in the region. The exact targets and outcomes of specific campaigns in 466 BCE are not clearly documented in surviving sources, but this period represents the height of Athenian naval expansion under Cimon's leadership before his ostracism.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Athenian naval expansion in the 460s BCE is well-established, but specific events in 466 BCE are not clearly documented. This entry represents the broader context rather than a specific documented event.
cross_references: Part of broader Delian League expansion

events.title: Possible dating of the Spartan earthquake and helot revolt
events.description: Some ancient sources place the major earthquake that struck Sparta and triggered the helot revolt in 466 BCE rather than 465 BCE. The chronological uncertainty reflects the difficulty in precisely dating events from ancient sources. The earthquake caused significant destruction and prompted a major helot uprising that would have major consequences for Spartan power and Greek geopolitics.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The earthquake is well-attested, but the precise year is uncertain. Different ancient sources place it in 466, 465, or 464 BCE. Geological evidence confirms major seismic activity in the mid-5th century but cannot pinpoint the exact year.

disconfirming_evidence: The year 466 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. Most events attributed to this year are either undated or have uncertain chronology. The Spartan earthquake is sometimes placed in 466 BCE, but other sources favor 465 or 464 BCE. The lack of specific, well-documented events for this year suggests that the historical record for 466 BCE is particularly thin, and many events assigned to this year may be approximations or reconstructions rather than precisely dated historical facts.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 466 BCE is notably sparse. Most surviving sources focus on major political and military events, and the year 466 BCE does not feature prominently in any major ancient narrative. The dating of events in this period depends heavily on the reconstruction of the Athenian archon list and synchronization with other chronological systems. There is a significant risk of anachronism or misplacement of events in the surviving sources. The Western bias toward Greek and Roman history is particularly pronounced for this year, with virtually no documentation from other regions.

graph_edges.note: The Spartan earthquake may have occurred in either 466 or 465 BCE; these entries represent alternative datings of the same event

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-5th century BCE, during the period of Athenian expansion and consolidation of the Delian League. Cimon is at the height of his power and influence in Athens, leading successful military campaigns. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is not yet in direct conflict with Athens. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes I is stable but facing periodic rebellions in its peripheral territories. This is a period of relative stability before the major conflicts that would define the later 5th century.

events.title: Athenian victory at the Battle of Eurymedon
events.description: Cimon led an Athenian naval and land force to victory against a combined Persian fleet and army at the Eurymedon River in Pamphylia. This battle is considered one of the most significant Athenian victories against the Persians and marked the effective end of major Persian naval operations in the eastern Mediterranean. The victory greatly enhanced Athenian prestige and power, solidifying Cimon's position as the leading figure in Athens. However, the precise dating of this battle is one of the most disputed questions in Classical chronology, with scholars proposing dates ranging from 469 to 465 BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Eurymedon is well-attested in ancient sources, but its dating is highly uncertain. Ancient sources do not provide a precise date, and modern scholars have proposed dates ranging from 469 to 465 BCE. The date of 467 BCE is one of several plausible reconstructions, but it is not definitively established. Some scholars favor 466 BCE, others 469 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Athenian expansion against Persia
Led to increased Athenian dominance in the Aegean

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Eurymedon is one of the most contentious issues in Classical Greek chronology. While traditionally placed in the 460s BCE, modern scholarship is deeply divided. Some scholars argue for 469 BCE, others for 466 BCE, and still others for 465 BCE. The ancient sources themselves do not provide a precise date, and the reconstruction depends on synchronization with other chronological systems (particularly the Athenian archon list) that are themselves uncertain. The significance of the battle is undisputed, but its precise historical placement remains highly problematic. Some scholars have questioned whether the battle occurred as described in the sources, though this view is minority opinion.
historiographic_note: The year 467 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. The only event of potential significance is the Battle of Eurymedon, but even this is not securely dated to 467 BCE. The historical record for this year is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Thucydides and Plutarch, both writing long after the events. There is virtually no documentation from Persian, Egyptian, or other non-Greek perspectives. The dating of events in this period is highly uncertain and depends on the reconstruction of the Athenian archon list, which itself contains significant gaps and uncertainties. The Western bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the absence of documentation from other regions means that major events in Asia, Africa, and elsewhere are likely completely unrecorded in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Eurymedon may have occurred in 467, 466, or 465 BCE; the dating is uncertain

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-5th century BCE, during the period of Athenian expansion under Cimon. The Delian League is consolidating its power in the Aegean. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is not yet in direct conflict with Athens. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes I is facing periodic rebellions and challenges to its authority. This period represents a transitional phase between the immediate aftermath of the Persian Wars and the later conflicts that would define the Classical period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Egypt (limited sources for this specific year)
disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for the year 468 BCE in surviving sources. The year appears to be a gap in the historical record, with no major political, military, cultural, or religious events clearly attributed to this year by ancient sources.
historiographic_note: The year 468 BCE is essentially undocumented in surviving sources. No major events are recorded for this year in any ancient chronicle or historical work. This absence of documentation may reflect either the actual lack of significant events or, more likely, the fragmentary nature of the historical record for this period. The dating of events in the 460s BCE is highly uncertain, and many events that occurred in this decade may be misplaced or misdated in surviving sources. The Western bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the absence of documentation from other regions means that major events elsewhere are completely unrecorded. The year 468 BCE represents a gap in our knowledge rather than a year of historical insignificance.

era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-5th century BCE, during the period of Athenian expansion and consolidation of the Delian League. Cimon is at the height of his power and influence in Athens. Sparta remains the dominant land power in the Peloponnese. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes I is consolidating its rule after the succession crisis following Xerxes' death. This period represents the early Classical age, characterized by the rise of democratic institutions in Athens and the development of Classical Greek culture.

events.title: Possible dating of the Battle of Eurymedon
events.description: Some scholars place the Battle of Eurymedon, a major Athenian victory against Persian forces, in 469 BCE rather than in the 460s BCE. Cimon led an Athenian naval and land force to victory against a combined Persian fleet and army. This battle is considered one of the most significant Athenian victories against the Persians and marked the effective end of major Persian naval operations in the eastern Mediterranean. The victory greatly enhanced Athenian prestige and power.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Eurymedon is well-attested in ancient sources, but its dating is highly uncertain. Modern scholars have proposed dates ranging from 469 to 465 BCE. The date of 469 BCE is one of several plausible reconstructions, favored by some scholars who use different chronological systems.
cross_references: Part of broader Athenian expansion against Persia

events.title: Birth of Socrates
events.description: According to traditional dating, the philosopher Socrates was born in Athens in 469 BCE. Socrates would become one of the most influential figures in Western philosophy, though he left no written works. His teachings, preserved through the writings of his student Plato and others, fundamentally shaped Greek and Western philosophy. However, the exact date of Socrates' birth is uncertain, and some sources place it in 470 or 471 BCE.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The traditional date of Socrates' birth is 469 BCE, but this is based on later sources and chronological reconstruction. Ancient sources do not provide a precise birth date. Modern scholars generally accept 469 BCE as a conventional date, but the actual year may have been 470 or 471 BCE.
cross_references: Related to the development of Classical Greek philosophy
Led to the philosophical tradition that shaped Western thought

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Eurymedon to 469 BCE is disputed. While some scholars favor this date, others argue for 466, 465, or even 464 BCE. The ancient sources themselves do not provide a precise date, and the reconstruction depends on uncertain chronological systems. The birth date of Socrates is also uncertain; while 469 BCE is the traditional date, some sources suggest 470 or 471 BCE. The uncertainty reflects the difficulty in precisely dating events from ancient sources that were written long after the events occurred.
historiographic_note: The year 469 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. The two events listed here—the possible Battle of Eurymedon and the birth of Socrates—are both subject to significant chronological uncertainty. The historical record for this year is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Thucydides, Plutarch, and later compilations. There is virtually no documentation from Persian, Egyptian, or other non-Greek perspectives. The dating of events in this period is highly uncertain and depends on the reconstruction of the Athenian archon list and other chronological systems that are themselves uncertain. The Western bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the absence of documentation from other regions means that major events elsewhere are completely unrecorded in surviving sources.

era_context: Classical Greece in the aftermath of the Persian Wars. Athens under democratic leadership (post-Cleisthenes reforms) is consolidating power and beginning its rise to hegemony in the Aegean. The Delian League, nominally a defensive alliance against Persian resurgence, is forming or consolidating. Sparta remains the dominant land power in Greece. In China, the Spring and Autumn Period is transitioning toward the Warring States Period; regional kingdoms are fragmenting into competing powers. In India, the Buddha is in his final years or recently deceased (depending on chronology); early Buddhist communities are organizing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources for this period)

events.title: Consolidation of the Delian League
events.description: Following the Greek victory at Salamis (480 BCE) and Plataea (479 BCE), Athens and allied Greek states formalize the Delian League as a defensive alliance against Persian aggression. The league's treasury is established on the island of Delos. Over the following decades, Athens gradually transforms this alliance into an Athenian empire, moving the treasury to Athens itself (c. 454 BCE). By 470 BCE, the league structure is solidifying, with Athens emerging as the dominant naval power and de facto leader.
key_figures: Aristides the Just (Athenian statesman, organizer of the league)
Themistocles (Athenian general, though his influence was waning by this date)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.96–97

events.certainty_note: The exact date of the Delian League's formal establishment is debated; 477 BCE is often cited, but the league's structure and Athens' role were still evolving in 470 BCE. Thucydides provides the most detailed account, though written decades later.

events.title: Ostracism of Themistocles (traditional date)
events.description: Themistocles, the celebrated naval commander of the Persian Wars, is ostracized from Athens. The traditional date for his ostracism is 471 or 470 BCE, though some sources place it as late as 465 BCE. Ostracism was a democratic procedure allowing Athenians to vote to exile a prominent citizen for ten years without loss of property or legal status. Themistocles' ostracism reflects factional tensions in Athens and the rise of rival leaders like Cimon and Aristides. He eventually fled to Persia, where he was granted refuge by the Persian king.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.135–138

events.certainty_note: The date of Themistocles' ostracism is uncertain, with sources ranging from 471 to 465 BCE. Plutarch and Thucydides provide accounts, but both wrote centuries later. Archaeological evidence (ostraca from the Agora) confirms the practice but does not precisely date this instance.

events.title: Death of the Buddha (traditional date in some chronologies)
events.description: In some Buddhist chronological traditions, Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) dies in this year or nearby (dates range from -543 to -400 depending on tradition). The Buddha's death marks the end of his direct teaching and the beginning of the early Buddhist monastic community's independent development. Different Buddhist traditions preserve different dates: the Theravada tradition places his death around 544 BCE, while other traditions date it later. The uncertainty reflects the lack of contemporary written records and the symbolic importance of the Buddha's life in Buddhist tradition.
events.certainty_note: The Buddha's death date is one of the most disputed dates in ancient history. No contemporary written sources exist. Different Buddhist traditions preserve conflicting dates spanning over 140 years. The Theravada tradition's date of 544 BCE is widely used in scholarship, but this is a scholarly convention, not a historical fact. The date -470 is included here as one possibility within the range of traditional estimates.

events.title: Warring States Period consolidation in China
events.region: China, Yellow River and Yangtze valleys
events.description: By 470 BCE, the Spring and Autumn Period is ending and the Warring States Period is beginning. The Zhou Dynasty's nominal authority is collapsing; regional kingdoms (Qi, Chu, Zhao, Wei, Han, Yan, and others) are consolidating power and competing for hegemony. Warfare becomes more frequent and large-scale. Iron tools and weapons are becoming widespread, enabling larger armies and more intensive agriculture. The period sees rapid social, military, and intellectual change, including the rise of Confucianism, Daoism, and Legalism.
key_figures: Various regional rulers (specific names uncertain for this exact year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The transition from Spring and Autumn to Warring States is a historiographic periodization. The exact date varies by source (traditionally 475 BCE, but some scholars place it earlier or later). Archaeological evidence confirms the period's military and technological changes.
cross_references: Part of broader -500 to -200 period of Chinese fragmentation

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Themistocles' ostracism is highly disputed; some scholars argue it occurred as late as 465 BCE, not 470 BCE. The traditional narrative of the Delian League as a purely defensive alliance is complicated by modern scholarship showing Athenian imperialism was present from the league's inception, not a later development. The Buddha's death date is so uncertain that including it at -470 is somewhat arbitrary; it could equally be placed anywhere from -543 to -400 depending on which tradition is followed. No single 'correct' date exists.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 470 BCE is moderate but heavily weighted toward Greece and China. Greek sources are abundant but mostly written decades or centuries after the events (Thucydides, Plutarch, Aristotle). Chinese sources are similarly retrospective (Sima Qian wrote c. 100 BCE). For India, the Buddha's death is a matter of religious tradition rather than historical record. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Northern Europe have no written sources for this year. The surviving record reflects the biases of literate civilizations and the interests of later historians.

graph_edges.note: Delian League consolidation leads to eventual Athenian imperialism and treasury relocation
Themistocles' ostracism removes a rival to Cimon's faction
Buddha's death (in some traditions) is contemporary with Warring States consolidation in China

era_context: Classical Greece in the early consolidation phase following the Persian Wars. Athens is strengthening its naval dominance and democratic institutions. Sparta remains the leading land power but is increasingly challenged by Athenian ambitions. The Delian League is in its early years of operation. In China, the Spring and Autumn Period is transitioning to the Warring States Period. In India, early Buddhist communities are organizing after the Buddha's death (if dated to this period).

events.title: Cimon's rise to prominence in Athens
events.description: Cimon, son of the famous general Miltiades (victor at Marathon), emerges as the leading political and military figure in Athens during the early 470s BCE. Cimon advocates for continued alliance with Sparta and aggressive action against Persian forces in the eastern Mediterranean. He leads successful naval campaigns against Persian-held territories and becomes the dominant figure in Athenian politics, eclipsing rivals like Themistocles (who is ostracized around this time) and Aristides. Cimon's ascendancy represents a shift toward a more conservative, pro-Spartan faction in Athens.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.98–101

events.certainty_note: Cimon's rise is well-attested in later sources, though the exact chronology of his early career is uncertain. He was certainly prominent by the 470s BCE, but the precise year of his emergence as the dominant figure is not specified in surviving sources.

events.title: Naxos revolt and Athenian response
events.description: The island of Naxos, a member of the Delian League, attempts to withdraw from the alliance. Athens, under Cimon's leadership, responds by besieging and subduing Naxos, forcing it to remain in the league. This event marks an early instance of Athens using military force to maintain control over league members, foreshadowing the transformation of the Delian League into an Athenian empire. The date of this event is uncertain; some sources place it around 471 BCE, while others suggest 470 or 469 BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.98

events.certainty_note: The Naxos revolt is attested in Thucydides, but the date is uncertain, with sources suggesting 471–469 BCE. Thucydides places it early in the Delian League's history but does not give a precise date.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of Athenian imperialism

events.title: Continued Persian Wars in the eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Greek forces, primarily Athenian, continue military operations against Persian-held territories in the eastern Mediterranean. Cimon leads campaigns against Persian forces in Thrace, the Hellespont, and Asia Minor. These campaigns are part of the ongoing conflict between Greek city-states and the Persian Empire following the major battles of 480–479 BCE. The exact battles and dates of individual engagements in 471 BCE are not well-documented, but the general pattern of Greek offensive operations is clear from later historical accounts.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.98

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of continued Greek-Persian conflict is well-attested, but specific events in 471 BCE are not clearly documented. The chronology of Cimon's campaigns is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader -470s to -450s period of Greek-Persian conflict

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Naxos revolt is disputed; it may have occurred in 470, 469, or even 468 BCE rather than 471 BCE. The narrative of Cimon as a consistently pro-Spartan figure is complicated by later evidence showing his policies shifted over time. The extent of Athenian imperialism in the early 470s is debated; some scholars argue it was more gradual than the traditional narrative suggests.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 471 BCE is similar to 470 BCE: moderate for Greece, sparse for other regions. Greek sources are retrospective and often written by authors with political biases (Plutarch favored biographical narrative; Thucydides had his own interpretive framework). The surviving record emphasizes Athenian and Greek perspectives, with minimal information about Persian, Egyptian, or other contemporary civilizations. The year 471 BCE is not particularly well-documented even within Greek history; many events are dated to the 'early 470s' without precision.

events.title: Aeschylus' Persians performed at Athens
events.description: The Greek tragedian Aeschylus presents his play 'Persians' (Persai) at the City Dionysia festival in Athens. This is the only surviving Greek tragedy based on a contemporary historical event—the Persian Wars. The play dramatizes the Persian perspective on the Greek victory, particularly the Battle of Salamis, and was performed just eight years after the battle itself. The play won first prize at the festival. It provides valuable insight into how Greeks understood and commemorated the Persian Wars and reflects the cultural and political atmosphere of Athens in the early 470s BCE.
key_figures: Pericles (possibly the choregos, or wealthy patron, though this is debated)

sources.name: Argument to Aeschylus' Persians (ancient scholarly commentary)

events.certainty_note: The play Persians survives and is dated to 472 BCE by the ancient argument (didascalia) that accompanies it. This is one of the few events from this year that is precisely dated in surviving sources.
cross_references: Related to -480-001 (Battle of Salamis, subject of the play)
Part of broader cultural memory of Persian Wars

events.title: Consolidation of Delian League structure
events.description: The Delian League continues to consolidate its structure and operations. Member states are assessed for tribute or naval contributions. Athens is gradually increasing its control over league affairs. The league's treasury remains on Delos, but Athens is the de facto leader. The exact events of 472 BCE are not well-documented, but the general pattern of league consolidation is clear from later historical accounts.
key_figures: Aristides the Just (Athenian statesman, associated with league organization)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.96–97

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of league consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 472 BCE are not documented. The year 472 BCE is not particularly significant in surviving sources.
cross_references: Part of broader -470s period of Athenian expansion

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Aeschylus' Persians is given as 472 BCE in the ancient argument, but some modern scholars have questioned this date, suggesting it might be 473 or 471 BCE. However, the ancient tradition is strong and widely accepted. No major disconfirming evidence exists for the events listed, but the year 472 BCE is generally poorly documented in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 472 BCE is sparse. The only well-documented event is Aeschylus' Persians, which survives as a text. For other regions and aspects of Greek history, 472 BCE is not specifically documented; events are often dated to the 'early 470s' without precision. Chinese and Indian sources do not provide specific information for this year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward Athens and Greek cultural production.

events.title: Possible date of Themistocles' ostracism (alternative chronology)
events.description: Some sources place Themistocles' ostracism in 473 BCE rather than 471 or 470 BCE. The exact date is uncertain, with different ancient sources providing conflicting information. Themistocles' ostracism reflects factional tensions in Athens and the rise of rival leaders like Cimon. The uncertainty in dating reflects the lack of precise chronological records in ancient Athens and the retrospective nature of later historical accounts.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.135–138

events.certainty_note: The date of Themistocles' ostracism is highly uncertain, with sources ranging from 471 to 465 BCE. Different scholars prefer different dates. This entry reflects the uncertainty by noting 473 BCE as one possibility.

events.title: Continued Delian League operations
events.description: The Delian League continues its operations as a defensive alliance against Persian aggression. Member states contribute tribute or naval forces. Athens is gradually increasing its control over league affairs. The exact events of 473 BCE are not well-documented in surviving sources, but the general pattern of league consolidation and Athenian expansion is clear.

The year 473 BCE is generally poorly documented in surviving sources, and no major events are specifically attributed to this year in ancient sources.

These campaigns are part of the ongoing conflict between Greek city-states and the Persian Empire. The exact battles and dates of individual engagements in 474 BCE are not well-documented, but the general pattern of Greek offensive operations under Cimon's leadership is clear from later historical accounts. Cimon's military successes enhance his prestige and political power in Athens.

Regional kingdoms (Qi, Chu, Zhao, Wei, Han, Yan, and others) are consolidating power and competing for hegemony. Warfare is becoming more frequent and large-scale. Iron tools and weapons are becoming widespread. The period sees rapid social, military, and intellectual change. The exact events of 474 BCE are not well-documented in surviving sources, but the general pattern of political fragmentation and military competition is clear.

events.certainty_note: The Warring States Period is well-attested archaeologically and in later historical sources. However, specific events in 474 BCE are not documented in surviving sources.

events.title: Early Buddhist community organization
events.description: Early Buddhist communities are organizing and spreading in the Indian subcontinent. The exact events of 474 BCE are not documented, but the general pattern of Buddhist community development in the 5th century BCE is clear from later sources. Buddhist monasteries are being established, and the monastic rules (Vinaya) are being codified. The Buddha's teachings are being transmitted orally and gradually written down.
key_figures: Early Buddhist monks and nuns (names uncertain for this exact year)
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of early Buddhist community development is attested in later sources, but specific events in 474 BCE are not documented. The dating of early Buddhist history is uncertain.
cross_references: Related to -470-003 (Buddha's death, traditional date in some chronologies)
Part of broader -500 to -300 period of Buddhist development

disconfirming_evidence: The year 474 BCE is generally poorly documented in surviving sources. No major events are specifically attributed to this year in ancient sources. The events listed above are inferred from broader patterns rather than specific documentation for 474 BCE. The chronology of Cimon's campaigns is uncertain, and specific battles cannot be reliably dated to 474 BCE.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 474 BCE is sparse. No major events are specifically documented for this year in surviving sources. The year is often subsumed into the broader 'early 470s' or 'mid-5th century' period. The surviving record reflects the interests of later historians and the biases of Greek sources. Chinese sources (Shiji, Bamboo Annals) are retrospective compilations written centuries after the events. Indian sources are primarily religious texts with uncertain historical value. Other regions have no written sources for this year.

era_context: Classical Greece in the early decades following the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens is consolidating its naval dominance and beginning its rise to hegemonic power in the Aegean. The Delian League, nominally a defensive alliance, is forming or consolidating. Sparta remains the dominant land power in Greece. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is transitioning toward the Warring States period; regional kingdoms are fragmenting into smaller, competing polities. In Persia, Xerxes I rules the Achaemenid Empire.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (no written records for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman contact
Mesoamerica (limited archaeological dating precision for this specific year)

events.title: Cimon's campaigns in the Aegean and consolidation of Athenian naval power
events.description: Cimon, son of Miltiades, leads Athenian and allied forces in campaigns against Persian-held territories and pirate strongholds in the Aegean. These campaigns, part of the broader post-Persian Wars consolidation, strengthen Athenian control over the Delian League and establish Athens as the dominant naval power. The capture of Eion and operations against Thracian territories extend Athenian influence northward. These victories cement Cimon's political dominance at Athens and fund the city's growing imperial ambitions.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.98–99)

events.certainty_note: Cimon's campaigns are well-attested in later sources, but precise dating within the 470s BCE is uncertain. Thucydides places them after the Persian Wars but does not give exact years. Plutarch's biography is written ~600 years after the events. The year 475 is conventional assignment based on historiographic consensus.
cross_references: Part of broader Delian League consolidation (-478 onwards)

events.title: Possible founding or formal reorganization of the Delian League
events.description: The Delian League, a defensive alliance of Greek city-states against Persian aggression, is either founded or formally reorganized around this time. The league's treasury is initially kept on the island of Delos, hence the name. Athens provides the military leadership and gradually transforms the league into an instrument of Athenian imperial power. The exact date of the league's founding is disputed; some sources place it in 478 BCE, others in 477 BCE. By 475 BCE, it is certainly operational and expanding.
key_figures: Aristides the Just (Athenian statesman, associated with league organization)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.96–97)

events.certainty_note: The Delian League's founding is traditionally dated to 478 or 477 BCE by ancient sources, but the exact year is uncertain. Thucydides does not provide a precise date. Modern scholarship places it within a few years of the Persian Wars' end (480 BCE). Assignment to 475 BCE is conventional and may be off by 2–3 years.

events.title: Continued fragmentation of Chinese regional states during Spring and Autumn period
events.description: In China, the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BCE) is in its final decades. The nominal Zhou dynasty has lost effective control; regional kingdoms (particularly Qi, Chu, Jin, and Qin) compete for dominance. Warfare is becoming more frequent and organized. The period is characterized by the rise of powerful feudal lords (ba, or 'hegemons') who claim to act in the Zhou king's name while pursuing their own territorial ambitions. This era produces the philosophical schools of Confucianism and Daoism in response to the chaos.
key_figures: Duke Jing of Qi (ruler of Qi state)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals), traditional dating to Confucius

events.certainty_note: The Spring and Autumn period is well-documented in Chinese chronicles, but specific events for individual years are often legendary or symbolic. The Shiji was compiled ~400 years after these events. Archaeological evidence confirms the period's general characteristics but rarely pinpoints specific years.
cross_references: -476-002 (traditional end of Spring and Autumn period)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and significance of Cimon's early campaigns are debated. Some modern scholars argue that the major campaigns traditionally assigned to the 470s BCE may have occurred later, in the 460s BCE, based on reassessment of Thucydidean chronology. The Delian League's founding date is contested: Thucydides suggests 478 BCE, but some scholars propose 477 BCE or even later. The league's transformation from a defensive alliance to an Athenian empire was gradual, not sudden, and attributing it to a single year is anachronistic. The Spring and Autumn period's end date (traditionally 476 BCE) is itself a historiographic convention; the transition to the Warring States period was gradual, not a discrete event.
historiographic_note: For Classical Greece, sources are relatively abundant but written centuries after the events (Thucydides ~50 years later, Plutarch ~600 years later). The bias toward Athenian and elite perspectives is pronounced; we know far less about non-Athenian Greek states or the experiences of non-elites. For China, the Shiji is the primary source, compiled during the Han dynasty (~400 years after the Spring and Autumn period). It blends historical record with legend and moral instruction. Archaeological evidence from both regions is fragmentary and does not always align with textual accounts. The year 475 BCE itself is a modern convention; ancient sources rarely provide precise year-by-year dating.

era_context: Classical Greece continues its post-Persian Wars consolidation. Athens is rising as a naval power under Cimon's leadership. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is beginning to face challenges to its hegemony. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is traditionally dated to end in 476 BCE, marking the transition to the Warring States period, though this is a historiographic convention rather than a discrete event. The Achaemenid Persian Empire under Xerxes I continues to dominate the Near East and western Asia.

events.title: Athenian naval operations and consolidation of Delian League power
events.description: Cimon continues Athenian naval campaigns against Persian-held territories and pirate strongholds. The Delian League's military operations are now well-established, with Athens providing the dominant naval force. These campaigns extend Athenian influence and extract tribute from allied states, gradually transforming the league into an Athenian-dominated empire. The year 476 BCE marks no specific major battle or event, but rather the continuation of a process that began in the 480s BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.98–99)

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Athenian expansion is well-attested, but specific events for 476 BCE are not clearly distinguished in sources. The year is assigned by convention.

events.title: Traditional end of the Spring and Autumn period in China; transition to Warring States period
events.description: Chinese historiography traditionally marks 476 BCE as the end of the Spring and Autumn period (Chunqiu, 770–476 BCE) and the beginning of the Warring States period (Zhanguo, 475–221 BCE). This division is based on the Chunqiu chronicle and later interpretations. The transition reflects a shift from a nominal Zhou dynasty with regional lords to a system of openly competing, increasingly powerful states. Warfare becomes more frequent, armies larger, and political organization more centralized. However, this is a historiographic convention; the actual transition was gradual, occurring over decades.
key_figures: Confucius (philosopher, lived 551–479 BCE, died during this period)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The date 476 BCE is a historiographic convention, not based on a specific datable event. The Chunqiu ends in 479 BCE (some versions 476 BCE), and later historians used this as a dividing line. The actual transition from Spring and Autumn to Warring States was gradual and cannot be pinpointed to a single year.

events.title: Death of Confucius (traditional date: 479 BCE, alternative dating)
events.description: Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551–479 BCE) is traditionally said to have died in 479 BCE, though some sources place his death in 478 BCE or 476 BCE. His death marks the end of an era in Chinese intellectual history. Confucius was a teacher and philosopher who emphasized ritual propriety (li), filial piety, and moral cultivation. Though he had limited political influence during his lifetime, his teachings became foundational to Chinese civilization. His death occurs during the transition from the Spring and Autumn to the Warring States period, a time of increasing chaos that his philosophy sought to address.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Lunyu (Analects of Confucius), compiled by disciples
Zuozhuan (Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals)

events.certainty_note: Confucius's birth year (551 BCE) is relatively well-attested, but his death year is given as 479 BCE in most sources, with some variation (478 or 476 BCE). The Shiji was compiled ~400 years after his death. No contemporary records of his death survive. The year 479 BCE is the traditional date, but 476 BCE is sometimes cited.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Confucius's death is disputed. Most sources give 479 BCE, but some texts cite 478 or 476 BCE. The Shiji, the most authoritative source, states 479 BCE, but this was compiled 400 years after the fact. The 'end' of the Spring and Autumn period at 476 BCE is a historiographic convention, not a historical event; the transition to the Warring States period was gradual. Some modern scholars argue that the division between these periods is artificial and that the Warring States period actually began earlier, in the 5th century BCE, or later, in the 4th century BCE. The significance attributed to 476 BCE as a turning point in Chinese history is largely a product of later historiographic tradition, not contemporary evidence.
historiographic_note: For Greece, sources are secondary and written decades to centuries after events. For China, the Shiji is the primary source but was compiled 400 years after the Spring and Autumn period. The Chunqiu itself is a sparse chronicle, often cryptic, and later commentaries (Zuozhuan, Gongyang zhuan, Guliang zhuan) provide interpretations that may not reflect original intent. The dating of Confucius's life and death relies on these later sources and is not independently verifiable. The division of Chinese history into periods (Spring and Autumn, Warring States, etc.) is a historiographic framework imposed by later scholars, not a contemporary categorization.

graph_edges.note: The Warring States period lasted until 221 BCE, when Qin unified China
Confucius's teachings became increasingly influential after his death, shaping Chinese civilization
Athenian naval expansion continued, eventually leading to the transformation of the Delian League into an empire

era_context: Classical Greece is consolidating post-Persian Wars power structures. Athens is rising as a naval hegemon, and the Delian League is either being founded or formally organized around this time. Sparta remains the dominant land power. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is in its final years, with regional fragmentation accelerating. The Achaemenid Persian Empire under Xerxes I continues to dominate western Asia and the eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Possible founding or formal organization of the Delian League
events.description: The Delian League is traditionally dated to 478 or 477 BCE by ancient sources. This alliance of Greek city-states was formed to continue resistance against Persian aggression and to liberate Greek cities still under Persian control. The league's treasury was initially kept on the island of Delos, hence the name. Athens provided the military leadership and gradually transformed the league into an instrument of Athenian imperial power. The exact founding date is uncertain; Thucydides does not provide a precise year, and later sources vary. By 477 BCE, the league is certainly operational.
key_figures: Aristides the Just (Athenian statesman, associated with league organization)
Pausanias (Spartan general, initially involved in post-Persian Wars operations)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.96–97)

events.certainty_note: Ancient sources give 478 or 477 BCE for the league's founding, but the exact year is uncertain. Thucydides places it after the Persian Wars but does not specify a year. Modern scholarship generally accepts a date within 478–476 BCE. The assignment to 477 BCE is conventional and may be off by 1–2 years.

events.title: Cimon's campaign against Thracian territories and consolidation of Athenian naval dominance
events.description: Cimon leads Athenian forces in campaigns in Thrace and the northern Aegean, extending Athenian influence and control over strategic territories. These campaigns are part of the broader consolidation of Athenian power following the Persian Wars. The capture of Eion and operations against Thracian strongholds strengthen Athenian control of trade routes and establish Athens as the dominant naval power in the region. These victories enhance Cimon's political prestige at Athens.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.98)

events.certainty_note: Cimon's Thracian campaigns are attested in later sources, but precise dating is uncertain. Plutarch and Thucydides do not provide exact years. The campaigns are generally placed in the 470s BCE. Assignment to 477 BCE is conventional.

events.title: Continued fragmentation and warfare in Chinese states during late Spring and Autumn period
events.description: In China, regional states continue to compete for dominance as the Spring and Autumn period approaches its end. Warfare is becoming more frequent and organized. The nominal Zhou dynasty has lost effective control, and powerful feudal lords (ba) pursue their own territorial ambitions. The period is characterized by increasing centralization of power within individual states and the emergence of larger, more organized armies. This era of chaos and competition produces the philosophical schools of Confucianism and Daoism.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of fragmentation and warfare during the late Spring and Autumn period is well-attested in Chinese chronicles. However, specific events for 477 BCE are not clearly distinguished in surviving sources. The year is assigned by convention.

disconfirming_evidence: The Delian League's founding date is disputed. Thucydides does not provide a precise year; later sources vary between 478 and 477 BCE. Some modern scholars argue for 476 BCE or even later. The league's transformation from a defensive alliance to an Athenian empire was gradual, not sudden. Cimon's campaigns are attested in later sources but lack precise dating; some scholars argue that major campaigns traditionally assigned to the 470s BCE may have occurred later, in the 460s BCE. The Spring and Autumn period's end date is a historiographic convention, not a discrete historical event.
historiographic_note: For Greece, sources are secondary and written decades to centuries after events. Thucydides is the most reliable source but does not provide year-by-year precision. Plutarch's biographies, written ~600 years later, are valuable but often blend history with legend and moral instruction. For China, the Shiji is the primary source but was compiled 400 years after the Spring and Autumn period. The Chunqiu is a sparse chronicle, and later commentaries provide interpretations that may not reflect original intent. The dating of specific events in both regions is often uncertain, and the year 477 BCE itself is a modern convention.

graph_edges.note: The Delian League eventually became the Athenian Empire; treasury moved to Athens in 454 BCE
Cimon's campaigns continued to expand Athenian power throughout the 470s BCE
Fragmentation continued until the traditional end of the Spring and Autumn period

era_context: Classical Greece is consolidating post-Persian Wars power structures. The Delian League is being founded or formally organized around this time, marking a shift from Spartan to Athenian leadership in Greek affairs. Athens is rising as a naval hegemon. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is beginning to face challenges. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is in its final years. The Achaemenid Persian Empire under Xerxes I continues to dominate western Asia.

events.title: Founding or formal organization of the Delian League
events.description: The Delian League is traditionally dated to 478 BCE by Thucydides and other ancient sources. This alliance of Greek city-states was formed to continue resistance against Persian aggression and to liberate Greek cities still under Persian control. The league's treasury was initially kept on the island of Delos, hence the name. Athens provided the military leadership and gradually transformed the league into an instrument of Athenian imperial power. The founding of the league marks a crucial shift in Greek politics: leadership passes from Sparta (dominant in the Persian Wars) to Athens (dominant in the Aegean). This transition is facilitated by Spartan withdrawal from eastern Mediterranean affairs and Athenian naval superiority.
key_figures: Aristides the Just (Athenian statesman, associated with league organization and assessment of tribute)
Pausanias (Spartan general, whose controversial behavior in Byzantium may have prompted Spartan withdrawal)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.96–97)

events.certainty_note: Thucydides places the league's founding after the Persian Wars (480 BCE) but does not give a precise year. Later sources give 478 BCE, which is accepted by most modern scholars. However, some scholars argue for 477 BCE or 476 BCE. The year 478 BCE is the most widely accepted date but is not definitively confirmed by contemporary evidence.

events.title: Athenian naval operations and expansion in the Aegean
events.description: Following the founding of the Delian League, Athenian naval forces conduct operations to liberate Greek cities from Persian control and to suppress piracy in the Aegean. These campaigns extend Athenian influence and establish Athens as the dominant naval power in the region. The operations are coordinated through the league's structure, with Athens providing the military leadership and other states contributing ships, money, or troops. These campaigns are crucial to the transformation of the league from a defensive alliance into an Athenian-dominated empire.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.98–99)

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Athenian naval expansion is well-attested, but specific events for 478 BCE are not clearly distinguished in sources. The year is assigned by convention based on the league's founding.

events.title: Continued fragmentation of Chinese states and regional warfare
events.description: In China, regional states continue to compete for dominance as the Spring and Autumn period approaches its end. Warfare is becoming more frequent and organized. The nominal Zhou dynasty has lost effective control, and powerful feudal lords pursue their own territorial ambitions. The period is characterized by increasing centralization of power within individual states and the emergence of larger, more organized armies. This era of chaos and competition produces the philosophical schools of Confucianism and Daoism in response to the disorder.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of fragmentation and warfare during the late Spring and Autumn period is well-attested. However, specific events for 478 BCE are not clearly distinguished in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Delian League's founding date is traditionally given as 478 BCE by Thucydides, but some modern scholars argue for 477 BCE or 476 BCE based on reassessment of chronological evidence. Thucydides does not provide a precise year, and the date 478 BCE is inferred from context. The league's transformation from a defensive alliance to an Athenian empire was gradual, not sudden, and attributing it to a single founding moment is somewhat artificial. Some scholars argue that the league was never formally 'founded' but rather evolved gradually from informal cooperation. The Spring and Autumn period's end date is a historiographic convention, not a discrete historical event.
historiographic_note: For Greece, Thucydides is the most reliable source, but he does not provide year-by-year precision. Later sources (Plutarch, Diodorus) are valuable but written centuries after the events and often blend history with legend. For China, the Shiji is the primary source but was compiled 400 years after the Spring and Autumn period. The Chunqiu is a sparse chronicle, and later commentaries provide interpretations that may not reflect original intent. The dating of specific events in both regions is often uncertain. The year 478 BCE itself is a modern convention based on ancient sources, but the exact date is not definitively confirmed by contemporary evidence.

graph_edges.note: The Delian League eventually became the Athenian Empire; treasury moved to Athens in 454 BCE
The Delian League's transformation into an empire eventually led to the Peloponnesian War
Athenian naval expansion continued throughout the 470s BCE
Fragmentation continued until the traditional end of the Spring and Autumn period

era_context: Classical Greece is in the immediate aftermath of the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). The final major battles of the Persian Wars (Plataea and Mycale) occur in 479 BCE, marking the end of the Persian invasion of Greece. Athens and Sparta are at the height of their cooperation, though tensions are beginning to emerge. The Delian League will be founded within a few years. In China, Confucius dies in 479 BCE (traditional date), marking the end of an era in Chinese intellectual history. The Spring and Autumn period is in its final year or has just ended. The Achaemenid Persian Empire under Xerxes I continues to dominate western Asia.

events.title: Battle of Plataea and Battle of Mycale: Final major battles of the Persian Wars
events.description: The Battle of Plataea (479 BCE) is fought in Boeotia between a Greek alliance (primarily Spartans and Athenians) and the Persian army under Mardonius. The Greeks achieve a decisive victory, effectively ending the Persian invasion of Greece. On the same day or shortly thereafter, the Battle of Mycale is fought off the coast of Ionia, where Greek forces defeat a Persian fleet. These victories mark the end of the Persian Wars and establish Greek independence from Persian domination. The battles are celebrated as pivotal moments in Greek history, establishing the Greeks as victors over the mighty Persian Empire. The victory at Plataea is particularly significant as it demonstrates the effectiveness of Greek hoplite tactics and the unity of the Greek city-states.
